cards.front: What does ABCCCD stand for in paediatric emergency assessment?
cards.back:
A - Airway
B - Breathing
C - Circulation
C - Coma
C - Convulsion
D - Dehydration

cards.front: What does 3TPR stand for in paediatric assessment?
cards.back:
T - Tiny baby assessment
T - Temperature
T - Trauma
P - Pallor
P - Poisoning
P - Pain

cards.front: What are the three components of assessing if a child is breathing (Look-Listen-Feel)?
cards.back:
LOOK - Look to see if the chest is moving or if the child is active, talking or crying
LISTEN - Listen for any breath sounds
FEEL - Feel for breath at the nose or mouth

cards.front: What are the three airway obstruction sounds and what do they indicate?
cards.back:
Gurgling - Pooled secretions or blood in airway
Snoring (Stertor) - Soft palate obstruction
Stridor - Harsh sound indicating upper airway obstruction from airway narrowing

cards.front: What are the signs of respiratory distress in a child?
cards.back:
Increased work of breathing
Child tired and distressed
Fast breathing (respiratory rate measured in breaths per minute)
Difficulty talking, eating, or breastfeeding
Severe chest wall indrawing
Short grunting noise on breathing
Head nodding with every respiration

cards.front: What is central cyanosis and how is it assessed?
cards.back: Abnormally low level of oxygen in the blood causing bluish/purplish discoloration of the tongue, inside mouth, and skin. Can occur with severe anaemia. Assessed by looking at the mouth and tongue. O2 saturation <92% indicates low oxygen and emergency.

cards.front: What is the critical difference in head positioning for airway opening between infants and older children?
cards.back:
INFANTS: Upper airways easily obstructed due to small diameter and soft windpipe. Head should be in neutral position with nose up. Do NOT use excessive backward head tilt as it can compress and narrow the windpipe.

OLDER CHILDREN: Can use head tilt like adults. Place hand on forehead and apply light pressure for slight tilt.

BOTH: Always check for trauma first before tilting or moving head.

cards.front: Why are infants' airways particularly vulnerable to obstruction?
cards.back: Infants have small diameter airways and the windpipe is soft and can be compressed and narrowed if excessive backward head tilt is applied. This makes them prone to obstruction compared to older children.

cards.front: What is croup and what sounds does it cause?
cards.back: Croup is a common infectious cause of upper airway obstruction. It produces stridor - a harsh sound on breathing that indicates upper airway obstruction from airway narrowing.

cards.front: What common foreign bodies can lodge in the upper airway and what history should be taken?
cards.back: Coins and peanuts are common foreign bodies that can lodge in the upper airway. A good history should be taken to assess for aspiration risk. Foreign body aspiration is an emergency and expects respiratory failure - may be obstructive type, central type, or tiredness with pneumonia.

cards.front: What other conditions can cause upper airway obstruction besides croup?
cards.back: Upper airway obstruction can be caused by: croup (most common infectious cause), foreign body aspiration, epiglottitis, and anatomical obstruction (pooled secretions, blood, tongue position). Always take a good history to assess for foreign body aspiration risk, especially with coins and peanuts.

cards.front: After performing head tilt-chin lift or jaw thrust, what must you check?
cards.back: Check that the maneuver has been effective. Do not just do it once. Check again by using Look-Listen-Feel assessment - look for chest movement, listen for breath sounds, feel for breath at nose or mouth.

cards.front: What is a Yankaer suction and what are its advantages?
cards.back: Yankaer suction is a firm plastic suction tip with a large opening surrounded by a bulbous head. Advantages: allows effective suction without damaging surrounding tissue, provides more control when suctioning (rigid vs softer flexible suction catheter), longer and effective at removing secretions from the airway, prevents plugging.

cards.front: What should be checked before opening the airway in a trauma patient?
cards.back: Always check for head and neck trauma. Ask if child could have had any trauma to head/neck or a fall which could have damaged the cervical spine (c-spine). Look for bruises or other signs of trauma. Stabilize neck first if trauma is suspected.

cards.front: What is the jaw thrust maneuver and when is it used?
cards.back: Jaw thrust without head tilt is the safe airway opening technique when c-spine injury is possible. Method: place thumbs on the forehead and 2-3 fingers under the angle of the jaw on both sides and lift jaw upwards. Thumbs are used to keep the head in place to ensure neck is not moved during the process. Safe to use in all ages.

cards.front: What are the methods for neck immobilization in suspected c-spine injury?
cards.back: Three methods: By Hand - manually support and stabilize; Blocks - head blocks to prevent movement; Log Rolls - careful rolling technique for children with c-spine injury. Apply correct size rigid neck collar with child lying on back. Prevent neck from moving by supporting head with blocks, sandbags, rolled towels, or litre bags of IV fluids on each side. Do NOT tape child's head to bed/trolley (risk of vomiting/aspiration).

cards.front: What is the log roll technique and how is it performed?
cards.back: Log roll is a technique for carefully moving a child with c-spine injury without rotating, flexing, or extending the neck. Procedure: Senior person assumes responsibility and stands at top end of child. Hold child's head to keep neck straight and in line with body by placing fingers under angle of mandible with palms over parietal region. 2-3 assistants needed to roll child's body in line with head and neck upon senior's clear instruction. Further person examines back for injuries. NO per rectal exam in children.

cards.front: What is an oropharyngeal (OP) airway and what does it do?
cards.back: An oropharyngeal airway moves the tongue away from the back of the throat and opens the upper airway. It is curved to fit the throat and must be long enough to reach the airways leading to the lungs. Would usually only be tolerated in a child with low level of consciousness. Beware - may provoke vomiting.

cards.front: What is the Guedel airway and what sizes are available?
cards.back: Guedel airway is an oropharyngeal airway device. Available sizes: 000, 00, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 - each with a different colour for each size. Sizing measured from midpoint of incisors to angle of mandible. Oversized airway could obstruct the airway by depressing the epiglottis. Undersized airway allows tongue to obstruct.

cards.front: How is the Guedel airway inserted in infants versus older children?
cards.back:
INFANTS: Put in right way up with tongue depressor to avoid obstruction by tongue.

OLDER CHILDREN: Can insert like anaesthetists do but with care. Curved tip points upwards (without being obstructed by tongue) then rotated 180 degrees and passes under hard palate.

ALL CHILDREN: If child appears to have a gag reflex and begins to retch, remove the oropharyngeal airway immediately.

cards.front: What are the five checks for an Ambu bag (manual ventilation device)?
cards.back: Is it connected to O2? Does it have a blow-off valve? Does it fit properly? Does it leak? How fast is ventilation rate - 2 seconds per breath?

cards.front: What is an Ambu bag and how does it function?
cards.back: Ambu bag is a hand-held device that delivers positive pressure ventilation. Function: squeezable bag with one-way valve and face mask. Compress the bag (opens the valve, forces air through mask/artificial airway into lungs). Release the bag (closes valve, exhalation occurs passively through exhalation port). With exhalation, bag fills with oxygen or room air.

cards.front: What are the causes of chest not rising when using an Ambu bag?
cards.back:
Inappropriate head or jaw position
Mouth closed
Insufficient pressure applied
Mucus or blood obstructing airway
Foreign body present
Poor seal between mask and face

cards.front: What is the C-grip and when is it used?
cards.back: The C-grip is the appropriate technique for holding a mask in place during manual ventilation with an Ambu bag. Different mask types may be used, but must be used appropriately regardless of mask type. Proper mask fit is essential for effective ventilation.

cards.front: What common error can occur during manual ventilation with Ambu bag that prevents effective ventilation?
cards.back: Soft tissue compression under the chin. This can occur even when head position is OK, reservoir is present, oxygen is attached, and mask fits well. Must be avoided by proper hand positioning and technique.

cards.front: What is the ideal ventilation rate when manually ventilating a child with an Ambu bag?
cards.back: The ventilation rate should be 2 seconds per breath when manually ventilating with an Ambu bag. This allows adequate time for chest inflation and exhalation.

cards.front: When should an oropharyngeal (OP) airway be removed?
cards.back: Remove the oropharyngeal airway immediately if the child appears to have a gag reflex and begins to retch. A gag reflex indicates the child is regaining consciousness and the airway may no longer be tolerated.

cards.front: What are the abnormal respiratory sounds that indicate obstruction?
cards.back:
Stridor - Upper airway obstruction due to airway narrowing; high-pitched squeaky noise when baby breathes in (both inspiratory and expiratory parts of cycle)
Grunting - Generally means low oxygenation
Wheeze - Indicates obstruction

cards.front: What causes stridor and how is it distinguished from other obstruction sounds?
cards.back: Stridor is caused by upper airway obstruction and airway narrowing. Common causes: croup, foreign body (croup more common), epiglottitis. Produces a high-pitched squeaky noise heard during both inspiratory and expiratory parts of breathing. Distinguish from stertor (obstruction of the oropharynx and tongue).

cards.front: What does grunting indicate in a child's breathing?
cards.back: Grunting sound (short noise on breathing) generally means low oxygenation in the child. It is a sign of respiratory distress that requires assessment and intervention.

cards.front: What is the threshold for oxygen saturation that indicates low oxygen and emergency?
cards.back: O2 saturation <92% in room air indicates low oxygen levels and is an emergency requiring immediate intervention.

cards.front: What are the three main steps in managing breathing problems?
cards.back:
1. Manage the airway
2. Give oxygen
3. Keep warm

cards.front: When giving oxygen during resuscitation for respiratory or airway problems, what concentration should be used?
cards.back: Always give the highest concentration of oxygen possible during resuscitation for respiratory or airway problems until satisfied with the breathing and oxygenation of the child.

cards.front: What are the two main sources of oxygen in emergency settings?
cards.back:
1. Oxygen cylinders - Easy to use, requires flow meter and tubing, operates without electricity, but relatively expensive and maintaining constant supply is difficult.
2. Oxygen concentrators - Pump room air through zeolite canister to remove nitrogen and concentrate oxygen. Moderate cost, requires little maintenance, produces oxygen continuously at low cost. Requires continuous electrical supply. Gives about 5 L/min - best used with nasal prongs/catheters.

cards.front: What are the four recommended methods for oxygen delivery in emergency settings?
cards.back:
1. Face mask with reservoir bag (non-rebreather mask)
2. Venturi face mask
3. Nasal prongs
4. Nasal catheter

cards.front: What type of oxygen delivery gives the highest concentration and is used in emergency?
cards.back: Face mask with non-rebreather reservoir bag (non-rebreather mask) delivers the highest concentration of oxygen and should be used in emergency when highest concentration is needed. Exhaled air is directed through one-way valve in the mask, preventing inhalation of room air and re-inhalation of exhaled air. The valve with sufficient seal around nose and mouth allows for higher concentrations of oxygen.

cards.front: How do Venturi face masks work and what oxygen concentrations can they deliver?
cards.back: Venturi face mask - tube-shaped reservoir with Venturi fitting at the end that delivers different oxygen concentrations from 28-60% according to the colour of each Venturi fitting. Check the bottom of the Venturi fitting to see which oxygen percentage will be delivered. The percentage depends on having the correct flow rate of 100% oxygen to the oxygen tube fitting.

cards.front: What are the flow rate requirements for nasal prongs in infants and children?
cards.back: Nasal prongs - short tubes inserted into nostrils. Right size, right way up, right fixation. Keep nose clear of mucus which can block flow of oxygen. Using 100% oxygen: Flow rate 1 L/min in infants and 1-2 L/min in older children to deliver 30-35% oxygen concentration in the inspired air.

cards.front: What is a nasal catheter, what sizes are used, and what are the flow rates?
cards.back: Nasal catheter - made from nasogastric tube (NGT) or suction catheter size 6 or 8 Gauge (FG 8 if using catheter). Insert into either nostril and secure, connected to oxygen. Tip of catheter should not be visible below the uvula. Right size, right way up, right fixation. Flow rate: 0.5 L/min in infants and 1 L/min in children delivers 35-40% O2 concentration in inspired air if 100% oxygen is used.

cards.front: What is the oxygen concentration delivered by a non-rebreather (reservoir bag) mask?
cards.back: A non-rebreather mask with reservoir bag delivers the highest concentration of oxygen available, typically >80% when properly sealed. It is the preferred method for emergency oxygen delivery requiring maximum concentration.

cards.front: What oxygen concentrations are available from different Venturi face mask fittings?
cards.back: Venturi face masks deliver 28-60% oxygen concentrations depending on the colour code of the Venturi fitting used. Check the bottom of the Venturi fitting to identify which oxygen percentage it will deliver. The actual percentage delivered depends on correct flow rate of 100% oxygen to the fitting.

cards.front: What oxygen concentrations can be achieved with nasal prongs at different flow rates?
cards.back: Using 100% oxygen at flow rates of 1 L/min in infants and 1-2 L/min in older children, nasal prongs deliver 30-35% oxygen concentration in the inspired air. Keep nose clear of mucus to prevent obstruction of oxygen flow.

cards.front: What oxygen concentrations can be achieved with a nasal catheter?
cards.back: Using 100% oxygen at flow rates of 0.5 L/min in infants and 1 L/min in older children, a nasal catheter delivers 35-40% oxygen concentration in the inspired air. Tip of catheter should not be visible below the uvula.

cards.front: What is the typical output of an oxygen concentrator and what is its best use?
cards.back: An oxygen concentrator produces about 5 L/min of oxygen by pumping room air through a zeolite canister to remove nitrogen. This is best used with nasal prongs or catheters which do not require high flow rates to achieve adequate oxygen concentration.

cards.front: What is the first step in managing a choking child?
cards.back: Determine if the child is actually choking. If choking is likely, assess the effectiveness of the child's cough.

cards.front: What are the key assessment components for a choking child?
cards.back:
1. Assess effectiveness of cough (effective vs ineffective)
2. Assess consciousness (conscious vs unconscious)
3. This determines the approach to choking management

cards.front: What is the danger of a child with history of foreign body aspiration showing increasing respiratory distress?
cards.back: A child with history of aspiration of foreign body who shows increasing respiratory distress is in immediate danger of completely obstructing the airway. Attempts to remove the foreign body must be made instantly, but attempts should not push the object further in.

cards.front: Why is spontaneous cough more effective than external maneuvers in relieving choking obstruction?
cards.back: Spontaneous cough is more effective at relieving an obstruction than an externally imposed maneuver because the child's own muscle action is more effective. Therefore, encourage effective coughing and do not interrupt it with unnecessary interventions.

cards.front: What defines an effective cough in a choking child?
cards.back: An effective cough is recognized by: Child's ability to speak, ability to cry, ability to take breaths between coughs. If child can speak, cry, and breathe between coughs, the cough is effective and no intervention should be made. Assess child continuously.

cards.front: When should intervention NOT be made in a choking child with effective cough?
cards.back: No intervention should be made unless the cough becomes ineffective. Signs that cough is becoming ineffective: Cough becomes quieter and silent, child cannot cry, child cannot speak, child cannot take a breath, child becomes cyanosed, child starts to lose consciousness. Only then call for help and start intervention maneuvers.

cards.front: What is the management approach for choking child with ineffective cough?
cards.back: Cough is ineffective - action and intervention maneuvers are required immediately. Treatment depends on age of child. Specific maneuvers differ for infants vs older children (back blows, chest thrusts, abdominal thrusts/Heimlich).

cards.front: What should be done if a choking child becomes unconscious during choking maneuvers?
cards.back: If at any time the child becomes unconscious or stops breathing during choking maneuvers, discontinue the choking maneuvers immediately and commence ventilation with high-flow oxygen using bag and mask, plus perform cardiac compressions if no signs of circulation are present.

cards.front: What is the 2-finger chest compression technique for choking infant?
cards.back: 2-finger compression location: 1 finger breadth above the xiphisternum. Compression depth: 1/3 of chest diameter. Hard enough to cause a compression wave in the airway. Position: head down. Sequence: perform 5 compressions, then repeat. This forces air out of the lungs to dislodge the foreign body.

cards.front: What is the back blow technique for older children with choking?
cards.back: Back blow - performed in a bigger child to dislodge foreign body. Uses back blows directed at the back to force the diaphragm and create pressure to expel the object from the airway. Sequence: perform 5 back blows, then reassess.

cards.front: What is the abdominal thrust (Heimlich maneuver) technique for choking child?
cards.back: Abdominal thrust (Heimlich maneuver) - pulls fist up and back to force the diaphragm up and cause a compression wave to expel the foreign body from airway. Direction of pull is upward into the abdomen. Sequence: perform 5 thrusts, then reassess. Used in older children.

cards.front: What is the exact location for 2-finger chest thrusts in an infant with choking?
cards.back: For 2-finger chest thrusts in choking infant: locate 1 finger breadth ABOVE the xiphisternum (lower end of sternum). Compression depth should be 1/3 of the chest diameter, hard enough to cause a compression wave in the airway.

cards.front: What is the sequence and frequency of chest thrusts for a choking infant?
cards.back: Perform 5 chest thrusts with 2-finger compression technique. After 5 compressions, reassess and recycle. Keep the infant's head in a head-down position during chest thrusts to aid expulsion of the foreign body. Continue the cycle of 5 compressions until the foreign body is expelled or clinical status changes.

cards.front: What is the head position during choking maneuvers for an infant?
cards.back: Keep the infant's head DOWN during choking maneuvers (back blows and chest thrusts). This head-down position aids in the expulsion of the foreign body by gravity and helps maintain airway positioning.

cards.front: What is the sequence and frequency of back blows for a choking older child?
cards.back: Perform 5 back blows directed to the back of the child to force the diaphragm up and create a compression wave in the airway. After 5 back blows, reassess the child's condition. If the obstruction persists, continue with the next maneuver (chest thrusts or abdominal thrusts depending on age) and recycle.

cards.front: What is the sequence and frequency of abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) for a choking child?
cards.back: Perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) by pulling the fist up and back with force to elevate the diaphragm and create a compression wave. After 5 thrusts, reassess. The direction of pull is upward into the abdomen. Recycle through the maneuver sequence if obstruction persists. This maneuver is used in older children, NOT infants.

cards.front: What is the complete cycling sequence for managing an older child with choking and ineffective cough?
cards.back: Perform: 5 back blows, reassess → 5 chest/abdominal thrusts, reassess → repeat cycle. Continue this iterative cycle until the foreign body is expelled, the child can cough effectively, or the child loses consciousness (then begin resuscitation with high-flow oxygen bag and mask plus CPR if needed).

cards.front: What is the iterative approach for managing the choking child?
cards.back: The choking child management approach follows a repeating cycle: assess effectiveness of cough and consciousness level, then apply appropriate maneuvers based on findings. Reassess after each round of maneuvers. The approach repeats until the object is dislodged, child can cough effectively, or resuscitation becomes necessary.

cards.front: What is the complete management algorithm for a choking child?
cards.back:
1. Determine if choking is likely
2. Assess effectiveness of cough (effective vs ineffective) and consciousness level
3. If EFFECTIVE COUGH: observe, do not intervene, reassess continuously
4. If INEFFECTIVE COUGH: call for help and begin intervention maneuvers immediately
5. Age-specific maneuvers: infants (back blows + chest thrusts), older children (back blows + abdominal thrusts)
6. If child becomes unconscious: discontinue choking maneuvers and begin bag-mask ventilation + chest compressions if no signs of circulation
7. Repeat cycle of maneuvers (5 per technique) until object expelled or resuscitation becomes necessary

cards.front: What is the legal definition of a child?
cards.back: A person below the age of 18 years

cards.front: What are the key characteristics that define childhood?
cards.back: Childhood is characterized by growth, development, vulnerability, and dependency, influenced by inherent ability, family setting, and broader social context

cards.front: List the basic child needs according to the lecture
cards.back: Food, Water, Shelter, Protection from harm, Health care services, Family environment, Love, Stimulation, Not to be involved in armed conflict

cards.front: What is the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC)? When was it developed and implemented?
cards.back: Developed in 1989, Implemented in 1990. It recognizes unique status, needs & rights of children, with the preamble stating children need special care & protection, and defines a child as any person under 18 years of age

cards.front: When did RSA ratify the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child?
List the general principles of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
cards.back: Right to survival & development, Right to equality & non-discrimination, Duty to promote the best interests of children, Right to be listened to & taken seriously / Participation

cards.front: What are civil and political rights as they relate to children?
cards.back: Right to life, survival & development; Right to equality and non-discrimination; Right to dignity; Right to birth registration, name and nationality; Right to participation; Right to information; Right to freedom of expression; Right to privacy and confidentiality; Right to physical and psychological integrity; Right to access courts; Right to protection from abuse and exploitation

cards.front: What are socio-economic rights as they relate to children?
cards.back: Right to life, survival and development; Right to an adequate standard of living; Right to health and healthcare services; Right to social security incl social assistance; Right to education; Right to water and sanitation; Right to housing and shelter; Right to food and nutrition; Right to a clean unpolluted environment; Right to protection from neglect

cards.front: What rights are specific to children only?
cards.back: Right to rest, leisure and play; Right to family, parental or alternative care; Right to special protection within criminal justice system; Right to special protection for unaccompanied foreign children; Right to survival and development; Right to protection from abuse, neglect, maltreatment and exploitation; Right to have their best interests of paramount importance; Right to be heard and taken seriously; Right to guidance in manner respecting evolving capacities; Right not to be used in armed conflict

cards.front: When was the African Charter on the Rights & Welfare of the Child adopted and by whom?
cards.back: Adopted in 1996 by the OAU (Organization of African Unity)

cards.front: When did RSA ratify the African Charter on the Rights & Welfare of the Child?
How many states have ratified the African Charter on the Rights & Welfare of the Child?
cards.back: 38 states have ratified

cards.front: What is the purpose of the African Charter on the Rights & Welfare of the Child?
cards.back: Extends CRC articles to include issues specific to children's rights in Africa

cards.front: Cite SA Constitution Section 12(2)(c)
cards.back: Everyone has the right to bodily and psychological integrity, which includes the right not to be subjected to medical or scientific experiments without their informed consent

cards.front: Cite SA Constitution Section 27(1)(a)
cards.back: Everybody has the right to have access to health care service

cards.front: Cite SA Constitution Section 28(2)
cards.back: A child's best interests are of paramount importance in every matter concerning the child

cards.front: According to SA Bill of Rights Section 28, what rights does every child have?
cards.back: Family care or equivalent, Basic nutrition, shelter, health & social services, Protection, Not to work, Not to be detained, Legal representation, Not to participate in armed conflict

cards.front: What is the age of majority in South African law?
cards.back: 18 years

cards.front: At what age can a child obtain an identity document according to South African law?
cards.back: 16 years (Identification Act - s15)

cards.front: At what age can a child leave school in South Africa?
cards.back: 15 years Completion of Grade 9 (South African Schools Act - s3(1))

cards.front: At what age can a child legally work in South Africa?
cards.back: 15 years (Basic Conditions of Employment Act s43 & 55(6)(b))

cards.front: At what age can a child choose domicile in South African law?
cards.back: 18 years (Domicile Act - s1)

cards.front: What are the age thresholds for sexual consent in South African law?
cards.back: Incapable of consenting: 12 years; Capable of consenting: 16 years (Criminal Law (Sexual Offences and Related Matters) Amendment Act -s15 & s16 read with s1(1)(b) & s56(2))

cards.front: At what age can a child consent to medical treatment with maturity in South African law?
cards.back: 12 years with maturity

cards.front: At what age can a child terminate pregnancy in South African law?
cards.back: Any age (Choice on Termination of Pregnancy Act s5(2) & s5(3))

cards.front: At what age can a child donate blood in South African law?
cards.back: 14 years (Human Tissue Act - s8(aa))

cards.front: What is the requirement for surgical treatment consent for children above 12 years?
cards.back: Sufficient maturity and capacity

cards.front: What are the requirements for surgical treatment consent for children below 12 years or lacking capacity?
cards.back: Sufficient maturity and capacity AND assistance of parent

cards.front: What is the requirement for condoms/contraceptive access for children?
cards.back: Regardless of maturity or capacity; requires medical advice and examination

cards.front: What are the requirements for HIV testing for children above 12 years?
cards.back: Sufficient maturity and capacity; pre- and post test counselling for all ages

cards.front: What are the requirements for HIV testing for children below 12 years?
cards.back: Sufficient maturity but not capacity; pre- and post test counselling for all ages

cards.front: What is the minimum age for male circumcision and what is the requirement?
cards.back: Above 16 years; regardless of maturity or capacity; every male child can refuse circumcision

cards.front: What is required for general medical consent for children above 18 years?
cards.back: Consent of parent PLUS consent (assent) of minor

cards.front: What is required for general medical consent for children below 18 years?
cards.back: Sufficient maturity but not capacity; need to advise minor to consult parent

cards.front: What are the key principles for child rights in health settings?
cards.back: Recognise vulnerability (newborns, children & adults); Encourage holistic health care (play & stimulation); Ensure appropriate support; Create child friendly environment; Foster participation (information, assent, consent); Facilitate access to suitable services (vital registration, grants)

cards.front: What is the Social Assistance Act in South Africa?
cards.back: Controls grants, Targets the poor; Therefore most grants have a means test

cards.front: What types of grants are available for children in South Africa?
cards.back: Child support grant, Foster care grant, Care dependency grant, Temporary grant-in-aid, Social relief

cards.front: What is the purpose of the Child Support Grant?
cards.back: Poverty alleviation

cards.front: What are the eligibility criteria for the Child Support Grant?
cards.back: Child < 18 years of age; Caregiver > 18 years of age

cards.front: What are the income limits for the Child Support Grant?
cards.back: Single parent < R 48,000 pa; Married couple < R 96,000 pa

cards.front: What are the benefits of the Child Support Grant?
cards.back: Annual revision; All biological children but maximum of 6 non-biological children

cards.front: What are the requirements to apply for Child Support Grant?
cards.back: Proof of income, Marriage/divorce certificate, Proof of guardianship

cards.front: How does one apply for the Child Support Grant in South Africa?
cards.back: Apply through local DoW (Department of Welfare) office

cards.front: When does the Child Support Grant terminate?
cards.back: When child turns 18; Death of child or primary caretaker; If primary caretaker ceases to care for child

cards.front: What is the purpose of the Foster Care Grant?
cards.back: Ensure appropriate alternative care for children not cared for by their biological parents

cards.front: What are the eligibility criteria for the Foster Care Grant?
cards.back: Applicant and child must be resident in South Africa; court order indicating foster care status; foster parent must be SA citizen, permanent resident or refugee; child must remain in care of foster parent(s)

cards.front: What is the value of the Foster Care Grant?
What is the purpose of the Care Dependency Grant?
cards.back: Support parents limited in ability to work due to caring for child with severe disability

cards.front: What is the age requirement for the Care Dependency Grant?
cards.back: Child must be under 18 years

cards.front: What are the citizenship requirements for Care Dependency Grant applicants?
cards.back: Applicant must be SA citizen, permanent resident or refugee, and resident in South Africa

cards.front: What are the income limits for the Care Dependency Grant?
cards.back: Single Parent earn < R202,800; Family < R405,600

cards.front: What is the value of the Care Dependency Grant?
What are the aims of the Children's Act in South Africa?
cards.back: Support families to enable them to ensure their children's well-being; Prevent abuse and neglect of children; Ensure children in need of care and protection are provided appropriate care

cards.front: Why is the Children's Act important for health professionals?
cards.back: It is a one-stop-shop for child consent, medical treatment, surgical operations, HIV testing and disclosure, consent when child lacks legal capacity, age child can access contraceptives, mandatory reporting in abuse and neglect, definitions of child in need of care and protection, and definitions of caregiver

cards.front: What is the concept of progressive realisation in children's rights?
cards.back: Obligation to move as expeditiously & effectively as possible, with plans including goals, timeframes, responsible parties & funds, and criteria for assessment through reasonableness test

cards.front: What are the key components of the Reasonableness Test in assessing state's law, policy or programme for children's rights?
cards.back: Conceptualisation (design must support realisation), Comprehensive & coherent & coordinated, Appropriate financial & human resource allocation, Reasonably implemented, Transparent & known, Balanced & flexible to meet short, medium & long-term needs

cards.front: What is neonatal immunity characterized as?
cards.back: New and immature

cards.front: Define neonatal sepsis
cards.back: Clinical syndrome of bacteremia with systemic signs and symptoms of infection in the first 28 days of life

cards.front: What are the two main classifications of neonatal infections by timing?
cards.back: Congenital (antenatal acquisition) and Acquired (typically occurs later)

cards.front: What are the routes of congenital acquisition?
cards.back: Transplacental, Ascending, Intrapartum, Acquired from the birth canal

cards.front: What is the timeframe for early neonatal infection?
cards.back: Birth to 7 days

cards.front: What is the timeframe for late neonatal infection?
cards.back: 8 days to 28 days, acquired in hospital, home, or community

cards.front: Name the TORCH organisms causing congenital infection
cards.back: Toxoplasma, Other (parvovirus, varicella, hepatitis), Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, Herpes, HIV, Syphilis

cards.front: List gram-positive organisms causing acquired neonatal sepsis
cards.back: Group B Streptococcus (GBS), Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Listeria monocytogenes

cards.front: List gram-negative organisms causing acquired neonatal sepsis
cards.back: Escherichia coli (E. coli), Klebsiella

cards.front: What are the main risk factors for neonatal infection?
cards.back: Maternal infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis), preterm birth, immunological deficiencies, lack of IgM/IgA antibodies, skin barrier defects, prolonged healthcare facility stay, facility-related factors (overcrowding, poor hand-washing, understaffing)

cards.front: What specific maternal factors are early risk factors for neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Prematurity, Chorioamnionitis, PROM greater than 18 hours, Maternal colonization with Group B Streptococcus

cards.front: What are late risk factors for neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Prematurity, Invasive procedures, Indwelling devices, Ventilators, VP shunts

cards.front: Why are preterm neonates at higher risk for infection?
cards.back: Less capable of responding to infection due to immunological deficiencies, lack of IgM and IgA antibodies, and passive antibody transfer occurs later

cards.front: What clinical signs suggest neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Temperature instability, respiratory distress and apnoea, feeding problems and vomiting, lethargy and refusal to suckle, pallor, petechiae, mottling of skin, jaundice, poor perfusion, unstable blood glucose

cards.front: What are nonspecific investigations for neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Full Blood Count (FBC), Platelet count assessment, White cell count (normal 5-20), Immature to total (IT) neutrophil ratio greater than 0.2 is predictive, C Reactive protein

cards.front: What are specific culture investigations for neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Blood, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), urine, and superficial swabs

cards.front: What additional serology investigations are used for neonatal infection?
cards.back: Serology for congenital infection

cards.front: What radiological investigations are used for neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: X-rays of areas suspected to be affected

cards.front: What are the management considerations for neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Local versus systemic infection, congenital versus nosocomial, supportive care (fluid, electrolyte, glucose correction, respiratory support, cardiovascular support), and antimicrobial therapy

cards.front: What is the first-line antimicrobial therapy for suspected neonatal sepsis?
cards.back: Penicillin with or without aminoglycoside

cards.front: How long should proven bacterial septicaemia in a neonate be treated?
cards.back: 7 to 10 days

cards.front: How long should confirmed bacterial meningitis in a neonate be treated?
cards.back: 21 days

cards.front: What are prevention strategies for congenital infections?
cards.back: Improved antenatal care, syphilis testing with documentation during pregnancy and treatment if needed, PMTCT (Prevention of Mother-To-Child Transmission) with ARV fast-tracking, TB screening, education about respiratory and viral pathogen exposure

cards.front: What are prevention strategies for acquired and nosocomial neonatal infections?
cards.back: Early skin-to-skin contact with mother (Kangaroo Mother Care), exclusive breastfeeding, strict hand washing, aseptic technique during procedures, limit duration of ventilation with active weaning

cards.front: What is the significance of congenital infections in South African maternal and child health?
cards.back: Impact is significant; HIV is 3rd leading cause of maternal mortality in SA

cards.front: Which congenital infections are prioritized in South African neonatal care?
cards.back: HIV/PMTCT, Syphilis (commonest), TB, Hepatitis B, Malaria, Listeria monocytogenes, COVID-19

cards.front: What are the consequences of congenital tuberculosis in neonates?
cards.back: Congenital TB: 43.48% (range 33.9-52.6%) mortality

cards.front: What is the key message regarding congenital infections?
cards.back: Most are preventable and treatable, especially if early diagnosed, leading to cure and control; if one infection is present, must look for others

cards.front: What is the clinical presentation of HIV-exposed neonates in the nursery?
cards.back: Mostly asymptomatic neonates; may have other neonatal problems such as prematurity, meconium aspiration syndrome, or hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy

cards.front: What is the clinical presentation of syphilis in the nursery?
cards.back: Can be asymptomatic and missed if maternal status not checked; may become symptomatic later as late congenital syphilis

cards.front: What are the earliest clinical manifestations of early congenital syphilis?
cards.back: Pneumonia (respiratory distress), rash, and hepatomegaly; often one or more features present - must look for others including long bone X-ray findings

cards.front: What is late congenital syphilis?
cards.back: Presentation up to 5 years of age, represents a tragic lost opportunity, includes earlier features with permanent morbidity and difficult treatment

cards.front: What are the key maternal factors in presenting congenital infections in the nursery?
cards.back: Maternal history, exposure history, and disease state are key to recognizing congenital infections

cards.front: What are the DOH guidelines for syphilis prevention in pregnancy?
cards.back: Syphilis testing to be performed and checked during pregnancy with clear documentation of results and treatment if received

cards.front: What does PMTCT stand for?
cards.back: Prevention of Mother-To-Child Transmission

cards.front: What are the key components of the DOH 4-Pillar approach to HIV prevention?
cards.back: Pillar 1+2: Testing, couples counselling and partner testing, safe sex education (dual contraception), PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis), PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis), VMMC (Voluntary Medical Male Circumcision), management of discordant couples

cards.front: How should syphilis in pregnancy be managed in the postnatal period?
cards.back: As a notifiable condition requiring appropriate follow-up and treatment

cards.front: What is the significance of maternal HIV testing frequency in PMTCT?
cards.back: Testing at first antenatal visit and ideally at every BANC (Basic Antenatal Care) visit, with ideal target of 8 visits for optimal screening

cards.front: What is PMTCT 2020 antenatal strategy for HIV management?
cards.back: Test, Test, Test at 1st visit and every BANC visit (ideal 8 visits)

cards.front: What is the PMTCT 2020 treatment strategy for HIV in pregnancy?
cards.back: Treat with Option B+ 2015: Start all HIV-positive pregnant women on ART as soon as possible; in labour if tested positive give TDF, 3TC, DLG (FDC) stat and continue lifelong next day with ART counselling and CHW referral

cards.front: What additional screening should be done during PMTCT antenatal care?
cards.back: Viral load monitoring, TB screening and other opportunistic infections (OI), and consider TPR (Treponema pallidum - syphilis testing)

cards.front: What are the components of syphilis screening in PMTCT 2020?
cards.back: Screen for syphilis and provide treatment (Rx) if indicated

cards.front: What is the peak mortality age of HIV-infected infants in South Africa?
cards.back: 2-4 months

cards.front: What is the significance of birth PCR (BPCR) implementation?
cards.back: Earlier diagnosis allows for initiation of treatment before peak mortality at 2-4 months

cards.front: What is the target timeframe for ART initiation in HIV-infected neonates with reactive BPCR?
cards.back: Initiation within 7 days of birth, or at 1st postnatal visit in primary health care settings

cards.front: What is the significance of maternal postnatal care in PMTCT?
cards.back: Opportunities to care for mother through postnatal check (CHW) and EPI (Expanded Programme on Immunization) visits

cards.front: What special considerations apply to HIV-exposed premature neonates less than 35 weeks gestation?
cards.back: Modified neonatal treatment regimens appropriate for prematurity and gestational age

cards.front: What is the follow-up protocol for HIV-exposed infants after birth?
cards.back: Structured follow-up based on PMTCT 2020 guidelines with assessment at key intervals

cards.front: What infections should be actively sought if congenital syphilis is diagnosed?
cards.back: HIV, TB, Hepatitis B, and malaria - if one congenital infection is present, must look for others

cards.front: What infections require specific laboratory tests and placental histology review?
cards.back: CMV (Cytomegalovirus), Rubella, Herpes, and Toxoplasmosis

cards.front: What is the comprehensive learning goal for neonatal congenital infections?
cards.back: Most congenital infections are preventable and treatable especially if early diagnosed with good outcomes (cure and control); if one infection is present must look for others

cards.front: What are the primary focus infections for neonatal care in South Africa?
cards.back: HIV/PMTCT and Syphilis (commonest causes)

cards.front: What infections require a structured clinical approach per DOH guidelines?
cards.back: TB, Hepatitis B (HBV), and Malaria

cards.front: What infections require knowledge of specific diagnostic tests and placental histology?
cards.back: CMV (Cytomegalovirus), Rubella, Herpes, and Toxoplasmosis

cards.front: What is the role of placental histology in neonatal infection diagnosis?
cards.back: Placental histology is important (NB) for diagnosis of congenital infections

cards.front: What is the reference standard for neonatal infection management in South Africa?
cards.back: 2020 Neonatal Guidelines and Drug Doses by Joolay and Horn (5th edition) and National DOH PMTCT Guideline 2020

cards.front: What is the highest peak age for accidental poisoning in children?
cards.back: 1–4 years is the highest peak age for accidental poisoning rates in children.

cards.front: What is the highest peak age for non-accidental poisoning in children?
cards.back: 15 years is the highest peak age for non-accidental poisoning.

cards.front: What is the gender distribution in poisoning incidents?
cards.back: Boys are more commonly affected than girls in poisoning incidents.

cards.front: What percentage of poisoning cases are fatal in children?
cards.back: Less than 2% of poisoning cases are fatal in children.

cards.front: Does the definition of poisoning in paediatrics include bites and envenomations?
cards.back: Yes, the definition of poisoning includes bites and envenomation.

cards.front: Where does poisoning rank among unintentional injury causes in high/middle income countries?
cards.back: In high and middle income countries, poisoning is the 4th biggest cause of unintentional injury after motor vehicle accidents (MVAs), fires, and drowning.

cards.front: At what ages is fatal poisoning rate highest in children?
cards.back: Fatal poisoning rate is highest for children under 1 year, with another peak at 15 years.

cards.front: What physiologic difference makes children under 4 months more vulnerable to toxins?
cards.back: Children under 4 months have increased skin and mucosal permeability to toxins.

cards.front: How does smaller body size affect toxin toxicity in children?
cards.back: Smaller body size in children means dose per kilogram is more toxic.

cards.front: How do decreased glycogen stores affect children's response to poisoning?
cards.back: Decreased glycogen stores in children may contribute to metabolic complications in poisoning.

cards.front: How does increased BSA create risk in paediatric poisoning?
cards.back: Increased body surface area (BSA) in children relative to body mass leads to thermoregulatory issues.

cards.front: List the main risk factor categories for childhood poisoning.
cards.back: Risk factors include: child factors, age, physical environment, and prevention strategies.

cards.front: Why are young children at high risk for accidental poisoning based on behaviour?
cards.back: Young children are inquisitive, unable to read warning labels, and imitate adult behaviour.

cards.front: What behavioural factors put adolescents at risk for non-accidental poisoning?
cards.back: Adolescents are at risk due to peer pressure and risk-taking behaviour despite being more aware of consequences.

cards.front: What are the four main primary prevention strategies for poisoning?
cards.back: Primary prevention strategies are: (1) removing poisoning agent from environment, (2) replacing poisonous agent with lower toxicity alternative (e.g., paracetamol versus aspirin), (3) legislation for child-resistant packaging, and (4) reducing toxicity by packaging in non-lethal concentrations or doses.

cards.front: What is the goal of secondary prevention in poisoning?
cards.back: Secondary prevention aims to prevent injury or illness once poisoning has occurred, using poisoning centres and methods of decontamination.

cards.front: What key information should be obtained in the history of a suspected poisoning case?
cards.back: Key history includes: what toxic agent/medicine was involved, what amount was ingested, route of exposure, when exposure occurred, any odours noted, whether child was alert on discovery, and history of substance abuse.

cards.front: What are toxidromes in the context of poisoning?
cards.back: Toxidromes are characteristic clinical syndromes that result from specific classes of toxins, allowing for recognition of poisoning patterns.

cards.front: What is a cholinergic toxidrome?
cards.back: Cholinergic toxidrome is a clinical syndrome resulting from excessive cholinergic stimulation, typically from organophosphates, carbamates, or muscarinic agents.

cards.front: What is an anticholinergic toxidrome?
cards.back: Anticholinergic toxidrome is a clinical syndrome from blockade of acetylcholine receptors, presenting with decreased parasympathetic activity.

cards.front: What is salicylism?
cards.back: Salicylism is the toxidrome resulting from salicylate poisoning (aspirin and related compounds).

cards.front: What is a sedative toxidrome?
cards.back: Sedative toxidrome is a clinical syndrome from CNS depressant poisoning, resulting in decreased consciousness and respiratory depression.

cards.front: What is a dystonic toxidrome?
cards.back: Dystonic toxidrome presents with dystonia (abnormal muscle tone and posturing) from agents like antipsychotics or metoclopramide.

cards.front: What is a sympathomimetic toxidrome?
cards.back: Sympathomimetic toxidrome results from sympathetic nervous system overstimulation, causing hypertension, tachycardia, and agitation.

cards.front: What is organophosphate poisoning?
cards.back: Organophosphate poisoning results from exposure to organophosphate compounds (pesticides), which irreversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase leading to cholinergic toxidrome.

cards.front: What does SLUDGE stand for in cholinergic poisoning?
cards.back: SLUDGE is a mnemonic for signs of cholinergic toxidrome: S = salivation, L = lacrimation, U = urination, D = diaphoresis, G = GIT upset, E = emesis.

cards.front: Describe the salivation sign in SLUDGE.
cards.back: Salivation (S in SLUDGE) refers to excessive drooling and salivary production in cholinergic poisoning.

cards.front: Describe the lacrimation sign in SLUDGE.
cards.back: Lacrimation (L in SLUDGE) refers to excessive tearing in cholinergic poisoning.

cards.front: Describe the urination sign in SLUDGE.
cards.back: Urination (U in SLUDGE) refers to involuntary urination (urinary incontinence) in cholinergic poisoning.

cards.front: Describe the diaphoresis sign in SLUDGE.
cards.back: Diaphoresis (D in SLUDGE) refers to profuse sweating in cholinergic poisoning.

cards.front: Describe the GIT upset sign in SLUDGE.
cards.back: GIT upset (G in SLUDGE) refers to gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, diarrhoea, and abdominal pain in cholinergic poisoning.

cards.front: Describe the emesis sign in SLUDGE.
cards.back: Emesis (E in SLUDGE) refers to vomiting in cholinergic poisoning.

cards.front: What is the ABC approach to organophosphate poisoning management?
cards.back: ABC refers to the initial management of organophosphate poisoning: Airway management, Breathing support, and Circulation control. Bradycardia, bronchorrhea, and bronchospasm are treated with atropine.

cards.front: What are the three respiratory symptoms of organophosphate poisoning that require atropine?
cards.back: Bradycardia, bronchorrhea (excessive bronchial secretions), and bronchospasm are the three respiratory symptoms of organophosphate poisoning requiring atropine treatment.

cards.front: How should atropine be administered in organophosphate poisoning?
cards.back: Atropine is given as a test dose, followed by repeat doses. Thereafter, follow up with continuous infusion.

cards.front: Why should atropine never be stopped abruptly in organophosphate poisoning?
cards.back: Atropine should never be stopped abruptly because symptoms will recur. Instead, wean atropine gradually over 24 hours.

cards.front: What complication should be watched for during atropine treatment of organophosphate poisoning?
cards.back: Seizures should be watched for during atropine treatment of organophosphate poisoning and treated accordingly.

cards.front: What is the main limitation of atropine in organophosphate poisoning treatment?
cards.back: Atropine has no effect on the paralysis caused by organophosphate poisoning.

cards.front: What is pralidoxime and how does it work in organophosphate poisoning?
cards.back: Pralidoxime is a cholinesterase reactivator used in organophosphate poisoning. It must be given within 48 hours of exposure to be effective.

cards.front: What is salicylate poisoning?
cards.back: Salicylate poisoning results from aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) or other salicylate-containing compound overdose.

cards.front: Describe Phase 1 of salicylate poisoning.
cards.back: Phase 1 of salicylate poisoning is characterized by hyperventilation due to direct stimulation of the respiratory centre, causing respiratory alkalosis with compensatory alkaluria (potassium and bicarbonate are lost). Phase 1 lasts as long as 12 hours.

cards.front: Describe Phase 2 of salicylate poisoning.
cards.back: Phase 2 of salicylate poisoning is characterized by paradoxic aciduria when sufficient potassium is lost. Phase 2 occurs hours to 24 hours after ingestion.

cards.front: Describe Phase 3 of salicylate poisoning.
cards.back: Phase 3 of salicylate poisoning is characterized by dehydration, hypokalaemia, and high anion gap metabolic acidosis. Phase 3 occurs 4–6 hours after ingestion in children.

cards.front: What are the neurological symptoms as salicylate toxicity progresses?
cards.back: As salicylate toxicity progresses, neurological symptoms include agitation, delirium, hallucinations, convulsions, lethargy, and stupor.

cards.front: What is hyperthermia in severe salicylate poisoning?
cards.back: Hyperthermia (elevated body temperature) occurs in severe salicylate toxicity.

cards.front: What metabolic complication occurs in salicylate poisoning?
cards.back: Hypoglycaemia (low blood glucose) can occur as a metabolic complication in salicylate poisoning.

cards.front: How is dehydration involved in salicylate poisoning?
cards.back: Dehydration occurs as part of the progression of salicylate poisoning, particularly in Phase 3.

cards.front: What does multiple organ involvement in salicylate poisoning refer to?
cards.back: Multiple organ involvement in salicylate poisoning refers to systemic effects affecting multiple body systems including respiratory, CNS, renal, and metabolic systems as toxicity progresses.

cards.front: What is iron poisoning?
cards.back: Iron poisoning results from overdose of iron supplements or iron-containing products, causing toxicity to multiple organ systems.

cards.front: What is the general toxicology of iron poisoning?
cards.back: Iron poisoning causes systemic toxicity through cellular damage, particularly affecting the gastrointestinal tract, cardiovascular system, and multiple organs.

cards.front: Why is iron particularly toxic in overdose?
cards.back: Iron is toxic in overdose because it causes direct corrosive injury to the gastrointestinal tract and generates free radicals that damage cellular structures.

cards.front: What are the main organ systems affected by iron poisoning?
cards.back: Iron poisoning affects the gastrointestinal tract (primary injury site), cardiovascular system, central nervous system, kidneys, and liver.

cards.front: What are the gastrointestinal effects of iron poisoning?
cards.back: Gastrointestinal effects of iron poisoning include vomiting (often with blood), abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and haemorrhage.

cards.front: What does paracetamol poisoning cause?
cards.back: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) poisoning causes hepatotoxicity leading to acute liver failure if not treated promptly.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of paracetamol toxicity?
cards.back: Paracetamol toxicity occurs when glutathione stores are depleted and the toxic metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinoneimine (NAPQI) accumulates, causing hepatocyte necrosis.

cards.front: What is the primary antidote for paracetamol poisoning?
cards.back: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the primary antidote for paracetamol poisoning, replenishing glutathione stores and preventing hepatotoxicity.

cards.front: What is the time window for NAC effectiveness in paracetamol poisoning?
cards.back: NAC is most effective when given within the first 8–10 hours after paracetamol ingestion, but can still provide benefit up to 24 hours or beyond.

cards.front: What are the phases of paracetamol poisoning?
cards.back: Phase 1 (0–24 hours): minimal symptoms. Phase 2 (24–72 hours): apparent improvement with hepatotoxicity developing. Phase 3 (72–96 hours): peak hepatotoxicity with jaundice, coagulopathy, and encephalopathy.

cards.front: What laboratory test guides paracetamol poisoning management?
cards.back: Serum paracetamol concentration is plotted on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram at specific times (4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24 hours post-ingestion) to determine hepatotoxicity risk and NAC need.

cards.front: What is the Rumack-Matthew nomogram used for?
cards.back: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is used to determine the risk of hepatotoxicity based on serum paracetamol concentration and time since ingestion, guiding treatment decisions in paracetamol poisoning.

cards.front: What supportive care is needed in paracetamol poisoning?
cards.back: Supportive care includes monitoring liver function tests, coagulation studies, and blood glucose; managing coagulopathy with fresh frozen plasma if needed; and monitoring for encephalopathy.

cards.front: What is the definition of caustic agents?
cards.back: Caustic agents are substances that cause chemical burns to tissues they contact, including strong acids and alkalis.

cards.front: What is the definition of corrosive agents?
cards.back: Corrosive agents are substances capable of corroding (destroying) organic tissue through chemical action, similar to caustic agents.

cards.front: What are examples of caustic/corrosive agents?
cards.back: Examples include strong acids (sulphuric acid, hydrochloric acid), strong alkalis (sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide), and other industrial chemicals.

cards.front: What areas of the body are affected by caustic/corrosive ingestion?
cards.back: Caustic/corrosive ingestion primarily affects the mouth, oesophagus, and stomach, with potential for full-thickness burns and perforation.

cards.front: What is the immediate management principle for caustic ingestion?
cards.back: Immediate management includes dilution with water or milk (do not induce vomiting), assessment for perforation, and urgent endoscopy to evaluate extent of damage.

cards.front: What are caustic and corrosive agents?
cards.back: Caustic and corrosive agents are chemical substances that destroy or damage organic tissue through direct chemical action. They include strong acids, strong alkalis, and other reactive chemicals.

cards.front: What is the clinical significance of caustic ingestion in children?
cards.back: Caustic ingestion in children is a serious emergency that can cause severe burns to the mouth, oesophagus, and stomach, potentially leading to perforation, mediastinitis, and death.

cards.front: What complications can result from caustic ingestion?
cards.back: Complications include oesophageal stricture formation, gastric perforation, mediastinitis, peritonitis, sepsis, and death if perforation occurs.

cards.front: What is the management approach for caustic ingestion in the acute phase?
cards.back: Acute management includes: (1) stop ingestion immediately, (2) do NOT induce vomiting or activated charcoal, (3) dilute with small sips of water or milk, (4) assess airway if burns involve mouth, (5) urgent endoscopy to assess depth of injury.

cards.front: Why is endoscopy essential in caustic ingestion?
cards.back: Endoscopy is essential to determine the extent and depth of tissue damage (superficial vs deep burns), assess perforation risk, and guide decisions on steroids, antibiotics, and surgical intervention.

cards.front: What is petrochemical poisoning?
cards.back: Petrochemical poisoning results from ingestion or inhalation of petroleum-derived products such as petrol (gasoline), paraffin, kerosene, or other hydrocarbons.

cards.front: What is the most common route of petrochemical poisoning in children?
cards.back: Ingestion is the most common route of petrochemical poisoning in children, often through accidental swallowing of petrol or paraffin.

cards.front: What are the systemic effects of petrochemical (hydrocarbon) poisoning?
cards.back: Systemic effects include gastrointestinal irritation (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea), CNS depression (lethargy, ataxia, loss of consciousness), and potential aspiration pneumonitis if aspirated.

cards.front: What is aspiration pneumonitis in hydrocarbon poisoning?
cards.back: Aspiration pneumonitis is a serious complication occurring when petroleum products are aspirated into the lungs, causing chemical pneumonitis with cough, dyspnoea, and potential respiratory failure.

cards.front: Why is vomiting particularly dangerous in petrochemical poisoning?
cards.back: Vomiting is dangerous in petrochemical poisoning because it increases the risk of aspiration of hydrocarbons into the lungs, causing chemical pneumonitis.

cards.front: What is the management approach for petrochemical ingestion?
cards.back: Management includes: (1) do NOT induce vomiting, (2) do NOT use activated charcoal, (3) supportive care with airway protection, (4) monitor for aspiration pneumonitis, (5) oxygen and mechanical ventilation if respiratory symptoms develop.

cards.front: What is the prognosis of aspiration pneumonitis from hydrocarbons?
cards.back: Most cases of hydrocarbon aspiration pneumonitis resolve with supportive care, but severe cases can progress to respiratory failure, ARDS, and death.

cards.front: What monitoring is needed after petrochemical ingestion?
cards.back: Monitoring includes observation for 6–12 hours for symptoms of aspiration (cough, dyspnoea, wheezing), chest X-ray to detect pneumonitis, and continuous pulse oximetry.

cards.front: What is the key principle in preventing accidental petrochemical poisoning?
cards.back: Prevention includes proper storage of petrochemical products in child-resistant containers, away from food containers, and out of reach of children.

cards.front: What is the general approach to any suspected poisoning in children?
cards.back: General approach includes: (1) ensure airway, breathing, circulation, (2) obtain detailed history of exposure, (3) identify the toxin, (4) assess toxidrome, (5) perform decontamination if appropriate, (6) administer antidotes if available, (7) supportive care, (8) monitor closely for complications.

cards.front: What role do poison control centres play in managing poisoning?
cards.back: Poison control centres provide expert consultation on the toxicity, management, and prognosis of poisonings. They offer guidance on specific antidotes, decontamination methods, and monitoring protocols.

cards.front: What decontamination methods are used in poisoning management?
cards.back: Decontamination methods include: (1) dilution (water/milk for caustic agents), (2) activated charcoal (for many toxins), (3) gastric lavage (for recent ingestions), (4) skin decontamination (washing with water), and (5) decontamination of eyes.

cards.front: What is the 2022 POISONING lecture series about?
cards.back: The 2022 POISONING lecture series, instructed by Wayne Rajah, covers epidemiology, pathophysiology, toxidromes, and management of poisoning in children across 44 slides, including organophosphates, salicylates, iron, paracetamol, caustic agents, and hydrocarbons.

cards.front: What is cyanosis?
cards.back:
Cyanosis is bluish discolouration of the skin and mucous membranes.
It results from either:
1. Excessive concentration of deoxygenated haemoglobin in blood, OR
2. Increased concentration of abnormal haemoglobin derivatives (e.g. methaemoglobinemia, sulphaemoglobinemia) in superficial blood vessels.

cards.front: Define central cyanosis
cards.back:
Central cyanosis is bluish discolouration of highly vascularized areas: lips, tongue, and mucous membranes.
Characteristics:
- Cardiac output and temperature usually normal
- Arterial oxygen saturation (SO₂) is reduced
- Generalized distribution
- Tongue is involved

cards.front: Define peripheral cyanosis
cards.back:
Peripheral cyanosis is bluish discolouration of distal extremities only.
Mechanism: Increased extraction of oxygen from sluggish blood movement through capillaries (e.g., from peripheral vasoconstriction or polycythaemia).
Characteristics:
- Systemic arterial oxygen saturation usually normal
- Localized distribution to distal extremities
- Tongue not involved

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: area of involvement
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: Generalized
Peripheral cyanosis: Localized

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: tongue involvement
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: Tongue involved
Peripheral cyanosis: Tongue not involved

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: temperature
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: Warm temperature
Peripheral cyanosis: Cold temperature

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: clubbing
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: Clubbing usually present
Peripheral cyanosis: Clubbing absent

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: response to oxygen supplementation
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: Improvement if pulmonary cause
Peripheral cyanosis: No improvement

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: response to warming
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: No improvement with warming
Peripheral cyanosis: Improvement with warming

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: mechanism
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: Reduction in oxygen saturation
Peripheral cyanosis: Reduction in blood flow

cards.front: Compare central and peripheral cyanosis: capillary refill time
cards.back:
Central cyanosis: < 2 seconds
Peripheral cyanosis: > 2 seconds

cards.front: List the 5 main pathophysiological mechanisms of cyanosis
cards.back:
1. Hypoventilation of the alveoli
2. Diffusion impairment
3. Ventilation-perfusion mismatch
4. Right to left shunting at the intracardiac or intrapulmonary vessels
5. Haemoglobinopathy that limits O₂ transport (e.g., methaemoglobin)

cards.front: What does the PALMS mnemonic represent for causes of central cyanosis?
cards.back:
P - Polycythaemia
A - Altitude
L - Lung
M - Methaemoglobin / Metabolic disorders (hypoglycaemia)
S - Shunt

cards.front: List pulmonary causes of cyanosis related to alveolar hypoventilation
cards.back:
A. Central nervous system depression: asphyxia, sedation, intracranial pathology
B. Neuromuscular disease: e.g. Guillain-Barré, Duchenne's muscular dystrophy
C. Airway obstruction: Choanal stenosis

cards.front: List causes of ventilation-perfusion mismatch
cards.back:
A. Extrinsic compression of the lungs: pneumothorax, pleural effusion, thoracic dystrophies
B. Intrinsic compression of the lungs: Pneumonia, lung atelectasis, hyaline membrane disease, asthma

cards.front: List causes of diffusion impairment in cyanosis
cards.back:
A. Pulmonary oedema
B. Bronchopulmonary dysplasia

cards.front: What cardiac causes lead to decreased pulmonary blood flow?
cards.back:
A. Tetralogy of Fallot
B. Tricuspid atresia

cards.front: What cardiac causes lead to increased pulmonary blood flow?
cards.back:
A. Transposition of the great vessels
B. Total anomalous pulmonary venous return
C. Truncus arteriosus

cards.front: What cardiac causes are associated with severe heart failure?
cards.back:
A. Coarctation of the aorta
B. Hypoplastic left heart

cards.front: List all cardiac causes of central cyanosis
cards.back:
1. Decreased pulmonary blood flow: Tetralogy of Fallot, Tricuspid atresia
2. Increased pulmonary blood flow: Transposition of great vessels, Total anomalous pulmonary venous return, Truncus arteriosus
3. Severe heart failure: Coarctation of the aorta, Hypoplastic left heart

cards.front: What is methaemoglobin and how can it cause cyanosis?
cards.back:
Methaemoglobin is an abnormal haemoglobin derivative.
It can cause cyanosis either from:
- Congenital methaemoglobinaemia, OR
- Secondary to toxin exposure

cards.front: What is sulphaemoglobin and how can it cause cyanosis?
cards.back:
Sulphaemoglobin is an abnormal haemoglobin derivative.
It causes cyanosis secondary to toxin exposure.

cards.front: What is the lecture topic and instructor?
cards.back:
Topic: CYANOSIS - "Blue child"
Instructor: Dr N. Mafanya

cards.front: What is tetralogy of Fallot?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis resulting from decreased pulmonary blood flow.
It is a cyanotic congenital heart defect.

cards.front: What is tricuspid atresia?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis resulting from decreased pulmonary blood flow.
It is a cyanotic congenital heart defect.

cards.front: What is transposition of the great vessels?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis resulting from increased pulmonary blood flow.
It is a cyanotic congenital heart defect.

cards.front: What is total anomalous pulmonary venous return?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis resulting from increased pulmonary blood flow.
It is a cyanotic congenital heart defect.

cards.front: What is truncus arteriosus?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis resulting from increased pulmonary blood flow.
It is a cyanotic congenital heart defect.

cards.front: What is coarctation of the aorta?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis associated with severe heart failure.
It is a cardiac lesion that can present with cyanosis.

cards.front: What is hypoplastic left heart?
cards.back:
A cardiac cause of central cyanosis associated with severe heart failure.
It is a cyanotic congenital heart defect.

cards.front: What are haemoglobinopathies that cause cyanosis?
cards.back:
1. Methaemoglobinaemia: congenital or secondary to toxin exposure
2. Sulphaemoglobaemia: secondary to toxin

cards.front: What is methaemoglobinaemia?
cards.back:
An abnormal haemoglobin derivative that causes cyanosis.
Can be either congenital or secondary to toxin exposure.

cards.front: What is sulphaemoglobaemia?
cards.back:
An abnormal haemoglobin derivative that causes cyanosis.
It occurs secondary to toxin exposure.

cards.front: What does the COLD mnemonic represent for causes of peripheral cyanosis?
cards.back:
C - Cold
O - Obstruction
L - LVF and shock
D - Decreased cardiac output

cards.front: What are the causes of peripheral cyanosis?
cards.back:
C - Cold (peripheral vasoconstriction)
O - Obstruction (to blood flow)
L - LVF (Left ventricular failure) and shock
D - Decreased cardiac output

cards.front: How does haemoglobin concentration affect the detection of cyanosis?
cards.back: Cyanosis from haemoglobin concentration is more apparent at high levels of saturation (high haemoglobin levels make cyanosis easier to detect).

cards.front: How does fetal haemoglobin affect the detection of cyanosis?
cards.back: Cyanosis related to fetal haemoglobin is more apparent at low levels of saturation.

cards.front: How does skin pigmentation affect the detection of cyanosis?
cards.back: Cyanosis is less apparent in patients with darker pigmentation, making detection more difficult in darker-skinned individuals.

cards.front: List all factors affecting the detection of cyanosis
cards.back:
1. Haemoglobin concentration: more apparent at high levels of saturation
2. Fetal haemoglobin: more apparent at low levels of saturation
3. Skin pigmentation: less apparent in patients with darker pigmentation

cards.front: What investigations should be performed in a cyanotic patient?
cards.back:
1. Saturation monitor
2. Hemoglobin or hematocrit
3. Arterial blood gas
4. Hyperoxia test
5. Septic screen
6. Electrocardiogram (ECG)
7. Echocardiography
8. Haemoglobin electrophoresis

cards.front: What does a saturation monitor measure?
cards.back:
A saturation monitor measures oxygen saturation (SO₂) - the percentage of haemoglobin that is oxygenated.
Used to assess the presence and degree of hypoxemia in cyanotic patients.

cards.front: What does haemoglobin or hematocrit measurement assess?
cards.back:
Measures the total haemoglobin concentration and red blood cell volume.
Important because higher haemoglobin concentrations increase the likelihood of detecting cyanosis.

cards.front: What is an arterial blood gas (ABG) test?
cards.back:
Measures:
- Partial pressure of oxygen (PaO₂)
- Partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO₂)
- pH
- Oxygen saturation (SaO₂)
- Bicarbonate levels
Essential for determining the cause of cyanosis (respiratory vs. cardiac vs. other).

cards.front: What is the hyperoxia test?
cards.back:
A test that helps differentiate between cardiac and pulmonary causes of cyanosis.
Patient is given high-concentration oxygen.
If oxygen saturation improves significantly, the cause is likely pulmonary; if not, cardiac cause is likely.

cards.front: What is a septic screen?
cards.back:
Investigations to rule out infection as a cause of cyanosis.
Includes blood cultures, complete blood count, and other tests to detect systemic infection.

cards.front: What does electrocardiography (ECG) assess in cyanotic patients?
cards.back:
Assesses:
- Cardiac rhythm
- Electrical conduction
- Evidence of chamber enlargement or ischemia
- Helps identify cardiac causes of cyanosis

cards.front: What does echocardiography assess?
cards.back:
An ultrasound examination of the heart that visualizes:
- Heart structure
- Valve function
- Blood flow patterns
- Chamber size and function
- Helps identify structural cardiac causes of cyanosis

cards.front: What does haemoglobin electrophoresis detect?
cards.back:
Separates and identifies different types of haemoglobin.
Used to diagnose haemoglobinopathies such as:
- Methaemoglobinaemia
- Sulphaemoglobinaemia
- Other abnormal haemoglobin variants

cards.front: What is the management of cyanosis?
cards.back:
1. Treat the underlying cause (address the primary condition)
2. Optimize oxygenation (supplemental oxygen, ventilation if needed)
3. Symptomatic management (supportive care)

cards.front: What is the primary approach to treating cyanosis?
cards.back: Treat the underlying cause - address the specific pathology causing the cyanosis (whether pulmonary, cardiac, or haemoglobinopathy).

cards.front: When was this lecture content last updated?
cards.back: 15 April 2020

cards.front: What is the definition of Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) in children?
cards.back: SAM is defined by any of the following: Weight-for-height/length <-3 SD; MUAC <11.5cm (for >6 months); Bilateral pitting oedema

cards.front: What is the hypoglycaemia threshold in SAM children?
cards.back: Low blood glucose is defined as <3 mmol/l

cards.front: How is asymptomatic hypoglycaemia (<3 mmol/l) treated orally in SAM?
cards.back: Immediate feed with F75, or sugar solution oral 10ml/kg, or 50ml bolus of 10% dextrose. Check blood glucose after 30 mins; if normal continue feeding, monitor 3 hourly. If low, continue to check every 30 minutes until >3 mmol/l

cards.front: What is the IV dextrose dosing for symptomatic hypoglycaemia in SAM?
cards.back: IV Dextrose 10%: 2 ml/kg over 2-3 mins. Check blood glucose after 30 mins; if normal continue feeding, monitor 3 hourly

cards.front: What is the normal axillary temperature range in children?
cards.back: Normal temperature: 36.5-37.4Â°C. Temp <36.5Â°C indicates urgent need to warm child

cards.front: What are the prevention measures for hypothermia in SAM?
cards.back: Keep covered, especially head, all the time. Avoid drafts. Keep dry, change wet napkins regularly. Avoid exposure during examination and bathing. Keep child in warm area (temp 25-30Â°C). Ensure regular correct feeding. Check axillary temperature 3-4 hourly

cards.front: What active rewarming measures are used for hypothermia in SAM?
cards.back: Skin-to-skin contact (Mother-Child). Wrap child with warm blanket. Place heater nearby. External warmer: heaters, hot-water bottle, bear huggers. During rewarming, monitor body temp every 30 mins. Temperature generally goes up 0.5-1Â°C per hour. Stop rewarming when body temp stabilized >36.5Â°C (prevent overheating)

cards.front: What are the grades of dehydration in SAM?
cards.back: No visible dehydration: normal presentation. Some dehydration: eyes sunken, slow skin pinch turgor (<2 sec), drinks eagerly, irritable/restless. Severe dehydration: eyes sunken, very slow skin pinch (>2 sec), drinks poorly

cards.front: How is diarrhoea without dehydration managed in SAM?
cards.back: Show mother how to give ORS with cup and spoon. Give frequent small sips. Give 10ml/kg after each loose stool until diarrhoea resolves. Continue to feed (F75). Reassess for dehydration frequently

cards.front: How is diarrhoea with some or severe dehydration managed in SAM?
cards.back: Give ORS 5ml/kg every 15 min (20 ml/kg/h) for 4 hours. Use frequent small sips. Continue to feed child. Reassess level of dehydration frequently. Review after 4 hours: general condition, capillary filling time, level of consciousness, skin turgor, sunken eyes, respiratory rate, abdomen, passing urine, number/quality of stools

cards.front: What are the IV fluid rates for treating dehydration with IV Darrows half-strength with dextrose 5% in SAM?
cards.back: 2-10 kg: 10ml/kg/hr. 11-20 kg: 8ml/kg/hr. 21-50 kg: 6ml/kg/hr. Given in addition to oral feeds at normal feed volumes and times

cards.front: What signs indicate shock in SAM children?
cards.back: Compensated shock: delayed capillary filling time >3 secs, increased pulse rate (>140-160 bpm), cool peripheries. Late (preterminal) shock: decreased level of consciousness (lethargic), decreased blood pressure, decreased pulse volume

cards.front: What is the fluid bolus dosing and administration for shock in SAM?
cards.back: Resuscitate with fluid bolus: 0.9% NS or Ringers lactate. Volume: 10ml/kg. Given as a bolus over 20 mins. Monitor for response (watch for fluid overload). If shock resolved, proceed to manage dehydration. If no response, repeat fluid bolus up to 3 times. After 3x10ml/kg boluses (total 30ml/kg) with inadequate response, move patient to high care bed for CVP monitoring and inotropic support

cards.front: What are the signs of fluid overload to monitor for during IV therapy in SAM?
cards.back: Further tachycardia, gallop rhythm, breathing increased even faster, puffy eyelids, enlarging liver size

cards.front: What is the Hb threshold for Very Severe Anaemia in SAM?
cards.back: Hb <4 g/dL

cards.front: What is the Hb range for Severe Anaemia in SAM?
cards.back: Hb 4-6 g/dL with respiratory distress

cards.front: What is the blood transfusion dosing and timing in SAM?
cards.back: Transfuse packed cells 5-10ml/kg slowly over 4 hours. Blood transfusions should only be given in the first 24 hours if a child has very severe anaemia or severe anaemia with respiratory distress

cards.front: What is the furosemide dosing during blood transfusion in SAM?
cards.back: Give furosemide 1mg/kg IV at the start and end of transfusion. Watch for signs of fluid overload

cards.front: What electrolyte abnormalities are found in SAM?
cards.back: All SAM children have excess body sodium even though plasma sodium may be low. Deficiency of potassium and magnesium. These can take up to 2 weeks to correct. Oedema partly due to these imbalances

cards.front: What is the critical principle for managing oedema in SAM?
cards.back: Do not treat oedema with a diuretic. Giving high sodium can kill

cards.front: What is the Gentamycin dosing regimen in SAM?
cards.back: Gentamycin IV 6mg/kg once daily for 7 days. Start on the first day of admission
