cards.front: What is the Amoxicillin dosing for SAM children with no danger signs?
cards.back: Amoxicillin oral 30mg/kg/dose 8 hourly for 5 days. For children with no danger signs, alert and feeding well

cards.front: What is the Ampicillin dosing for SAM children with danger signs?
cards.back: Ampicillin IV/IM 50mg/kg 6 hourly for 7 days. For all other children not meeting low-risk criteria. Avoid IV infusions if possible. Use heparin lock to avoid fluid overload. Hang time: 1 hour

cards.front: What antibiotic is used for SAM children severely ill or failing to improve after 48 hours?
cards.back: Third generation cephalosporin e.g. Ceftriaxone IV/IM 50mg/kg/dose once daily. If meningitis is suspected: 80-100mg/kg/dose

cards.front: What is the Mebendazole dosing for children 1-2 years in SAM?
cards.back: Mebendazole oral 100mg 12 hourly for 3 days. Given during rehabilitation phase

cards.front: What is the Mebendazole dosing for children >2 years in SAM?
cards.back: Mebendazole oral 500mg as a single dose immediately. Given during rehabilitation phase

cards.front: What is the Folic Acid dosing in SAM?
cards.back: Only give stat dose of 5mg folic acid on day 1. Do not give daily (already in F75, F100 and RUTF). If not on F75/F100 prescribe folic acid 2.5mg daily

cards.front: When is Iron supplementation started in SAM?
cards.back: Only give iron after the stabilization phase is completed (rehab phase). Iron supplementation is only given once the child is gaining weight and the oedema has resolved, usually after 7 days

cards.front: What is the Iron dosing in SAM during rehabilitation?
cards.back: Give 2mg/kg elemental iron per dose 8 hourly with meals when the child is on F100. Stop iron when child is taking full prescribed amount of RUTF

cards.front: What is the Vitamin A supplementation rule in SAM?
cards.back: Give single age specific high dose Vitamin A capsule if the child has severe measles and/or clinical signs of vitamin A deficiency. For children without the above complications and who are receiving F75, F100 or RUTF, no high dose vitamin A supplementation required

cards.front: What is the Zinc supplementation rule in SAM?
cards.back: Children with SAM receiving Ready-To-Use F75, F100 or RUTF should not be given additional zinc supplements even if they have diarrhoea, as these therapeutic feeds contain the recommended amounts of zinc for diarrhoea

cards.front: What is the F75 (Starter Formula) volume for SAM children without oedema?
cards.back: 130ml/kg/day over 8 feeds

cards.front: What is the F75 (Starter Formula) volume for SAM children with severe oedema?
cards.back: 100ml/kg/day over 8 feeds

cards.front: What are the F75 nutritional specifications?
cards.back: 100 kcal/kg/day; Protein 0.9 g/kg/day. Low-lactose feed

cards.front: What is RUTF and when is it introduced in SAM?
cards.back: RUTF (Ready-To-Use Therapeutic Feed) is an energy-dense, vitamin and mineral-enriched therapeutic food that is equivalent to F100. It is a groundnut paste composed of vegetable fat, peanut butter, skimmed milk powder, lactoserum, maltodextrin, sugar and mineral and vitamin complex. Start once taking well orally in rehabilitation phase. Start increasing volume by 20ml per day until some feed remains unfinished or reaches 220mls/kg

cards.front: How is the transition from F75 to F100/Infatrini managed in SAM?
cards.back: For the first day or two replace the stabilizing feed (F75) with rehabilitation feed (F100 or DF100) at the same volume. The transition phase should be gradual (2 days) to avoid the risk of heart failure

cards.front: What are the discharge criteria for SAM in children 0-6 months?
cards.back: Established breastfeeding. Bilateral pitting oedema resolved. Child clinically well and alert. Medical complications resolved. Persistent and good weight gain (>5g/kg/day) over 5 days

cards.front: What are the discharge criteria for SAM in children 6-59 months?
cards.back: Appetite returned, tolerating RUTF. Child clinically well and alert. Bilateral pitting oedema resolved. Medical complications resolved. Persistent and good weight gain (>10g/kg/day) over 5 days

cards.front: What investigations must be done before discharge in SAM?
cards.back: Investigate for TB. Repeat tuberculin skin test and read it within 48 hours. Record findings. Ensure HIV counselling and testing was done. Record findings. Ensure information on family background and socio-economic status was obtained and seen by social worker

cards.front: What follow-up schedule is recommended after SAM discharge?
cards.back: Follow up at 2 weeks or one month after discharge at POPD (Place Of Primary care Discharge). Dietetics review until growing well. Maintain malnutrition register and nutrition care plans. Contact details and address of patients. Follow up defaulting patients. Prevent readmissions

cards.front: What lactose intolerance diagnostic criteria are used in SAM?
cards.back: Intolerance should be diagnosed only if copious watery diarrhoea occurs promptly after milk-based feeds are begun, the diarrhoea clearly improves when milk intake is reduced or stopped, and it recurs when milk is given again. Other signs include acidic faeces (pH <5.0) and the presence of increased levels of reducing substances in the faeces

cards.front: What is the management of lactose intolerance in SAM?
cards.back: Substitute with an extensively hydrolyzed feed. Reintroduce milk feeds gradually in the rehabilitation phase

cards.front: What monitoring parameters are required during F75 feeding in SAM?
cards.back: Monitor and note: Amounts offered and leftover. Vomiting. Reoffer vomited amount. Frequency of watery stool. Daily body weight. During the stabilisation phase, diarrhoea should gradually diminish and oedematous children should lose weight

cards.front: What is the normal weight gain target during SAM rehabilitation?
cards.back: Moderate weight gain: >1-2 g/kg/day. This indicates good positive weight gain

cards.front: What breastfeeding guidance is given for exclusively breastfed SAM infants <6 months?
cards.back: If an exclusively breastfed baby is admitted as SAM, there is probably a problem with breastfeeding (low breastmilk supply/incorrect suck/latching) or the breastmilk is not enough to meet increased nutritional requirements. For these babies add F75 in addition to breastmilk regardless of HIV status. Calculate F75 as per stabilisation volume guidelines and breastfeed in-between

cards.front: What are the absolute contraindications to defer feeding in SAM?
cards.back: Defer feeding immediately in following cases: While shock is being corrected. Surgical abdominal emergency

cards.front: What organs are at risk in SAM and what feeding principles protect them?
cards.back: Heart: avoid excess fluid. Give the correct volume of feed. Kidneys: avoid excess fluid and excess sodium. Give the correct volume of feed and give a low sodium feed. Liver: avoid excess protein and provide adequate glucose. Give low protein feed, give sugary feed, feed frequently. Gut: avoid large meals. Give small amounts, feed frequently

cards.front: What is the TB diagnostic approach in SAM?
cards.back: Actively investigate for TB as soon as possible. TB is difficult to diagnose and confirm. Ask about contacts and symptoms. Do Tuberculin Skin Test (TST) and chest x-ray. If TST negative, repeat just before discharge. If TB is clinically likely, presumptive TB treatment is reasonable, but once begun should be completed

cards.front: What is the HIV management in SAM?
cards.back: Counsel mother/caregiver and test for HIV. Once the child enters the rehabilitation phase, commence ART as per provincial guidelines. HIV infected children with SAM should be closely monitored in the first 6-8 weeks of initiation of ART

cards.front: What sensory stimulation and development is promoted during SAM rehabilitation?
cards.back: Aspects of child development to be promoted: Cognitive skills, Language skills, Motor skills, Exploratory skills, Social skills. All SAM patients must be referred to occupational therapy and speech therapy in the rehabilitation phase. All SAM patients must be referred to a social worker

cards.front: What special cases require continued inpatient care after rehabilitation phase in SAM?
cards.back: Children remaining due to another medical condition. Unable to eat RUTF and ward diet. Social cases

cards.front: What is SAM malnutrition classification (6-59 months)?
cards.back: MUAC <11.5cm OR weight-for-height <-3 SD OR bilateral pitting oedema

cards.front: What is Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM) classification?
cards.back: Either of the following: Weight-for-height/length -2 and >-3 SD, OR MUAC between 11.6 and 12.5cm

cards.front: What SAM danger signs require immediate intervention?
cards.back: Hypoglycaemia, Hypothermia, Hypovolaemia, Septic shock (severe sepsis), Severe dehydration (not in shock), Severe anaemia, Eye signs of Vitamin A deficiency

cards.front: What are the 8 types of child abuse?
cards.back:
1. Physical abuse
2. Sexual abuse
3. Emotional/psychological abuse
4. Medical neglect
5. Nutritional neglect
6. Intentional drugging
7. Safety neglect
8. Organised abuse

cards.front: How is physical abuse determined clinically?
cards.back: Physical abuse is determined by the pattern, age and distribution of injuries. Typical features include: delay in seeking help, discordance between history and injury, help sought from multiple/different healthcare professionals, inappropriate health seeking behaviour, and presenting to different facilities at different times (e.g., late at night).

cards.front: What are the red flags for physical abuse presentation?
cards.back: Red flags include: delay in seeking help, discordance between the history and the injury, help being sought from multiple/different healthcare professionals (inappropriate health seeking behaviour), and presenting to different health facilities at different times (e.g., late at night).

cards.front: List 7 causes and predisposing factors for physical abuse by caregivers.
cards.back:
1. Parents/caretakers who were abused themselves as children
2. Alcoholism and drug abuse
3. Severe psychological problems (present in ~10% of abuse cases)
4. Breakdown of family structures
5. Social isolation
6. Poverty (including wealthy families)
7. Unemployment

cards.front: Which children are at highest risk of physical abuse?
cards.back: Children at risk have: behavioural problems (ADHD, Conduct Disorder, Oppositional Defiant Disorder), learning difficulties, neurodevelopmental disorders (Autism Spectrum Disorder, speech/language delay, visual/hearing impairment), or chronic illnesses (neurological, liver, renal, cardiac).

cards.front: What is the average age of a child being physically abused and the average age at fatality?
cards.back: Average age of child being abused: approximately 7 years. Average age of fatality in physical abuse: 3 years.

cards.front: Is physical abuse more common in males or females?
cards.back: Males are more commonly abused than females (M > F).

cards.front: What is the age distribution of physically abused children?
cards.back: 1/3 of abused children are < 6 months old, 1/3 are between 3 months and 3 years, and 1/3 are > 3 years of age.

cards.front: How does child age affect the risk of severe injury and death in physical abuse?
cards.back: The smaller the child, the greater the risk of severe injury and death. Physical abuse is a common cause of child deaths.

cards.front: List 7 differential diagnoses to consider when evaluating for physical abuse.
cards.back:
1. Birth trauma
2. Congenital syphilis
3. Osteogenesis imperfecta
4. Rickets
5. Scurvy (poor wound healing, bleeding gums)
6. Bleeding disorders (haemophilia, meningococcaemia, ITP)
7. Skin diseases (impetigo, chickenpox, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome)

cards.front: Define non-accidental injury and contrast with accidental injury.
cards.back: Non-accidental injury: deliberate action by a perpetrator which intentionally threatens and inflicts physical harm to a child. Accidental injury: unforeseen events that cause external trauma (e.g., fall from height, run over by motor vehicle, hit by object, burnt by water or fire). Key difference: there should be NO discordance between history and injury in accidental cases.

cards.front: Describe the historical timeline of child abuse from antiquity to the 19th century.
cards.back: Antiquity (372 AD): Infanticide. 4th-13th Century: Abandonment. 13th-19th Century: Ambivalent & Intrusive period, Exploitation & abuse.

cards.front: What are the main modes of inflicting blunt trauma in physical abuse?
cards.back: Blunt object trauma may be inflicted using: fist, bat, elbow, wooden spoon. Clinical presentation: haematoma, swelling, bruising, subconjunctival haemorrhage.

cards.front: What implements are used to inflict whipping injuries in non-accidental injury?
cards.back: Whipping may be inflicted with: hosepipe, sjambok, stick/twig, wet face cloth.

cards.front: Describe the types of burn injuries used in non-accidental injury.
cards.back: Two types: (1) Glove and stocking burns from immersion in hot water. (2) Branding injuries from hot metallic objects.

cards.front: What are the clinical features of blunt trauma in physical abuse?
cards.back: Clinical features include: haematoma, swelling, bruising, and subconjunctival haemorrhage.

cards.front: What are the risk factors and presentation in the case of QM, an 8-year-old female with secondary nocturnal enuresis?
cards.back: Risk factors: chronically unwell parent (Steven Johnson Syndrome with visual impairment, verbally abusive), marital problems with ongoing parental conflict, father alcoholic and physically abusive, social disadvantage (renting one room with other occupants). Presentation: secondary nocturnal enuresis, learning difficulties, symptoms of depression. Child disclosed physical abuse from both parents.

cards.front: What were the key findings in the case of AM, a 10-year-old male with dysmorphic features?
cards.back: Social factors: marital conflict, deceased father (COVID-19) who was physically abusive, passive mother. Medical history: dysmorphic child, poor concentration, easily distracted, 'zoning out in class', known epileptic on KEPPRA and EPILIM. Admission findings: 24-hour EEG, labs (LFTs, ammonia, drug levels, FBC, U&E, creatinine). Diagnosis: breakthrough seizures and difficult-to-control epilepsy. Disclosure: child disclosed physical abuse from teacher (throwing books, hitting).

cards.front: What was the clinical presentation and outcome in the case of the 12-year-old male with perinatal asphyxia?
cards.back: Background: ex-premature infant with perinatal asphyxia (APGAR 4-6/10), ventilated in NICU for 2 weeks, neonatal jaundice (phototherapy), feeding problems, seizures (phenobarbitone), repeated grades 4-5. No vision or hearing assessment, no SIAS form. Context: professional nurse mother and deputy director father. Outcome: parents disclosed physically abusing the child. Management: medical management and referral to educational psychologist after counselling.

cards.front: What are the members of the multidisciplinary team in managing physical abuse?
cards.back: The multidisciplinary team includes: Psychologist, Social worker, Medical doctor, Nurse, Teachers.

cards.front: What are the long-term psychological complications of physical abuse in children?
cards.back: Long-term complications include: Anxiety, Depression, Mood disorders, Personality disorders. Additionally, survivors of physical abuse may themselves become abusers.

cards.front: What are Kempe's and Faller's definitions of child sexual abuse?
cards.back: Kempe: Involvement of a child in sexual activity without consent, without understanding, contrary to norms of society. Faller: Sexual activity involving a child in which there is a power imbalance.

cards.front: What is the epidemiology of child sexual abuse regarding gender and age?
cards.back: Prevalence: 1:3 females, 1:5 males affected. Age peak in females: < 6 years and early teens. Age peak in males: late in first decade. Affects all social classes, races, and religions.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of perpetrators of child sexual abuse?
cards.back: Over 80% of children are abused by someone known to them (2/3 are family members, often the primary caregiver). Perpetrators are typically: financially or otherwise stressed, with drug or alcohol abuse, underachievers with low self-esteem, jealous/manipulative personality (more than frank mental disorders), history of abuse themselves. Males represent > 95% of perpetrators, often as series of groomers and abusers.

cards.front: What are the behavioural indicators of sexual abuse in Child X?
cards.back: Behavioural indicators: poor concentration, learning difficulties, poor social skills (unable to make friends), 'autistic features', bullied at school (grades 1-3), extremely anxious, American accent, high-pitched feminine voice, obese, attached to females, fearful of males and detached from father. Diagnosis: depression and generalised anxiety disorder. History: early maternal separation, intact family but both parents extremely busy professionals.

cards.front: What are the behavioural indicators in Child Y, a 9-year-old male referred for learning difficulties?
cards.back: Behavioural indicators: moved from school to school due to 'behavioural problems', poor social skills, hyperactivity, stealing, inappropriately touching other children. Family context: mother is a nurse aware of the abuse but provided no intervention.

cards.front: List the physical symptoms and signs of child sexual abuse.
cards.back: Symptoms: Recurrent abdominal pain, Difficulty walking or sitting, Painful micturition and recurrent UTIs, Secondary enuresis, faecal soiling or retention. Signs: Discharge from penis or vagina, Abnormal dilatation of vagina or anus, Genital laceration/bruising, Vaginal bleeding, Signs of STIs.

cards.front: What is the J88 form in the context of child sexual abuse?
cards.back: The J88 is a medico-legal examination form completed by healthcare providers documenting: relevant history of sexual offence, details of medico-legal examination, clinical findings, gynaecological examination findings and conclusions, and diagrams of findings.

cards.front: What is the Adams Classification?
cards.back: The Adams Classification describes the pattern of injuries in child sexual abuse, specifically genital findings, to standardize documentation and interpretation of examination findings.

cards.front: What are the differential diagnoses to consider in suspected child sexual abuse?
cards.back: Differential diagnoses: Bleeding disorders, Straddle injuries, Anaesthesia (causing anal dilatation), Conscious sedation, Vulvovaginitis (from worm infestation, poor hygiene, yeast infection, prolapsed anus from acute gastroenteritis, dermatological conditions like lichen sclerosus et atrophicus or eczema, harsh soaps, allergies, chlorine from swimming pool).

cards.front: What is the key principle in evaluating straddle injuries as differential diagnosis?
cards.back: There must be consistency between the history and the injury in straddle injuries. Ask: How did the injury occur? Common mechanisms include bicycle seat, palisade fence, concrete wall, car handbrake, shopping trolley, or broom handle.

cards.front: What are the suspicious circumstances that raise concern for sexual abuse?
cards.back: Suspicious circumstances include: Unexplained injuries, Discrepant histories, Delay in seeking health care, Alleged self-inflicted injuries, Alleged third party inflicted injuries, Repeated injuries, Sexualised behaviour, Sexually transmitted infection.

cards.front: Why is it important to observe behavioural changes closely in abused children?
cards.back: Any abused child is likely to have behavioural problems. Behaviours should be observed closely by experienced people. Asking about behavioural problems in the history is essential as behaviour often reveals important clues about the nature of the child abuse.

cards.front: What are the prevention and treatment approaches for post-traumatic stress disorder in sexually abused children?
cards.back: Prevention: Debriefing via social worker for disclosure and psychologist for counselling—the earlier the better. Treatment: Recognize signs (sleep disturbance, change in appetite, separation anxiety, school performance deterioration, general behavioural changes including hypervigilance, mood lability, sexualized behaviour) and provide appropriate intervention.

cards.front: What are the six stages of child abuse accommodation syndrome?
cards.back:
1. Helplessness
2. Coercion to secrecy
3. Entrapment
4. Accommodative behaviour
5. Disclosure (delayed and unconvincing)
6. Recantation

cards.front: What are the members of the multidisciplinary team managing child sexual abuse?
cards.back: The multidisciplinary team includes: Social worker, Psychologist, Medical doctor, Nurse, Police officers (Child protection unit), Victim assistant officers, Counsellors, School authorities (teachers/principals), Court representatives.

cards.front: What are the four main roles of healthcare workers in managing child abuse?
cards.back:
1. Recognise abuse
2. Accurately document extent of clinical findings (physical, psychological)
3. Provide appropriate treatment
4. Report case to relevant authorities

cards.front: What key elements must be included when taking a history of suspected non-accidental injury?
cards.back: Essential elements: How injury happened (informants, dates, times, place, sequence of events, people present), interview child separately without leading questions, ask about the abuse and perpetrator(s), document child's psychological state (crying, calm, distressed, withdrawn), Mental status examination for PTSD.

cards.front: What additional history must be obtained when evaluating for non-accidental injury?
cards.back: Birth history and pregnancy, Growth, Development, Immunisations, Systemic enquiry, Family and social history, School information (creche, preschool, grades passed/failed/condoned), Teacher reports (Conner's, request for assistance forms).

cards.front: What key aspects must be documented during examination for non-accidental injury?
cards.back: For bruises: size, shape, position, colour and age must be recorded. Examine the whole child including oral cavity and genitals. For acute cases < 72 hours: consider EUA (examination under anaesthesia) if uncooperative or perineal injury present.

cards.front: What are the key procedural requirements for examining a child with suspected sexual abuse?
cards.back: Requirements: Privacy must be maintained. Examination by skilled medical officer. Third person must be present. Procedure must be explained to child. All samples and photographs taken BEFORE patient is cleaned. Full general examination performed. Small children examined on parent's lap; older children supine in lithotomy position.

cards.front: What are the key findings and documentation principles for vaginal examination in suspected sexual abuse?
cards.back: Key principles: Lack of evidence does not mean abuse has not taken place. Normal crescentic hymen is the most common finding in prepubertal girls. Total absence of hymen is extremely rare as a normal variant. Tears of hymen may leave clefts or bumps when they heal. Document findings using the hands of a clock as reference.

cards.front: What is the assessment triad for sexual abuse evaluation?
cards.back: The triad consists of: Hear (listen for disparity between history and injury, delay in seeking help), See (observe behavioural changes and recurrent injuries), Find (identify a pattern of injuries).

cards.front: What findings on anal examination require careful interpretation in suspected sexual abuse?
cards.back: Anal dilatation can result from: childhood sexual abuse, stool in rectum, constipation, Crohn's disease, recurrent rectal prolapse, anaesthesia, or sedatives. Anal fissures may indicate: constipation or abuse (look for fresh and/or multiple tears). Anal skin tags are the result of healed anal fissures. Document mucocutaneous changes and complete dilatation. Perform examination in lateral decubitus position and note stage of sexual development.

cards.front: What imaging is required in suspected physical abuse?
cards.back: Every physically abused child < 5 years old requires imaging. Skeletal survey includes: AP skull, AP chest, AP spine, AP arms/legs. CT-scan is indicated for: suspected head injury, medico-legal purposes, screening for previous injuries. Ophthalmology consultation for retinoscopy to assess for flame-shaped retinal haemorrhages.

cards.front: When should photographs be taken in child abuse cases?
cards.back: Photographs are indicated in severe sexual abuse cases to document physical signs. Digital photography is acceptable; prints must be made immediately.

cards.front: What is post-exposure prophylaxis and when is it indicated in child sexual abuse?
cards.back: Post-exposure prophylaxis includes: Antibiotics (for STI prevention), Pregnancy prevention measures (when indicated by age and menstrual status), Treatment of injuries evident on presentation.

cards.front: Why must siblings be assessed when child abuse is identified?
cards.back: 20% of siblings will also be involved in abuse at some stage. All children in homes and places of safety must be included. They should be referred as soon as possible for assessment and intervention.

cards.front: Which professional groups are at risk of not recognizing abuse in their own children and why?
cards.back: High-risk professionals include: Nurses, Doctors, Social workers, Lawyers, Police officers, Soldiers. These professionals work long hours, spend long periods away from families, and do not easily recognize signs of abuse in their own children.

cards.front: What are the long-term complications of child sexual abuse?
cards.back: Long-term complications include: Mood disorders, Personality disorders, Depression, Anxiety disorders. The prognosis in survivors with underlying mental illness and a history of childhood sexual assault is guarded.

cards.front: What are the major challenges in managing child sexual abuse cases?
cards.back: Challenges include: Fragmented management and lack of private units managing sexual assault cases, overloaded public sector hospitals, lack of skilled healthcare workers and training, lack of support for families, high default rate, lack of places of safety for children, long waiting times between patient contact and court appearances, safety concerns for doctors/caregivers/children, burnout and lack of support for staff (feeling outnumbered and outgunned), lack of adherence to guidelines.

cards.front: What are the key conclusions about child abuse management?
cards.back: Child abuse is very common. All healthcare workers must be adept and confident at recognizing and managing cases. Management must be holistic. The aim of effective management is to prevent secondary trauma to survivors and their families.

cards.front: Normal respiratory rate (RR) for Newborn?
cards.back: 40 - 50 breaths per minute

cards.front: Normal respiratory rate (RR) for Infants?
cards.back: 25 - 40 breaths per minute

cards.front: Normal respiratory rate (RR) for 1-5 years?
cards.back: 20 - 30 breaths per minute

cards.front: Normal respiratory rate (RR) for 5-10 years?
cards.back: 15 - 25 breaths per minute

cards.front: Normal respiratory rate (RR) for >10 years?
cards.back: 15 - 20 breaths per minute

cards.front: What respiratory condition causes Bark cough?
cards.back: subglottic obstruction

cards.front: What respiratory condition causes Brassy cough?
cards.back: tracheal compression

cards.front: What respiratory condition causes Paroxysmal cough with whoop?
cards.back: pertussis

cards.front: What is Pectus excavatum?
cards.back: caved-in or sunken appearance of the chest. Associated with Marfan syndrome.

cards.front: What is Pectus carinatum?
cards.back: protrusion of the sternum and ribs. Associated with Noonan syndrome.

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Tracheal tug?
cards.back: Tracheal tug indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Supraclavicular recession?
cards.back: Supraclavicular recession indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Intercostal recession?
cards.back: Intercostal recession indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Subcostal recession?
cards.back: Subcostal recession indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Nasal flaring?
cards.back: Nasal flaring indicates accessory muscles

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Abdominal breathing?
cards.back: Abdominal breathing indicates accessory muscles

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Head bobbing?
cards.back: Head bobbing indicates accessory muscles - secondary to sternocleidomastoid contractions

cards.front: What general sign indicates increased work of breathing?
cards.back: Difficulty speaking or feeding, expiratory grunting, restlessness and agitation, drowsiness, cyanosis

cards.front: What are the main symptoms of acute tonsillitis?
cards.back: Sore throat, fever, and pain on swallowing

cards.front: What are the stages of tonsillar inflammation?
cards.back: 1) Parenchymatous stage - uniformly inflamed, hyperaemic tonsils; 2) Follicular stage - yellow exudate in tonsillar crypts; 3) Membranous stage - exudate coalesces to form membrane

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for acute tonsillitis?
cards.back: Penicillin or Amoxycillin for 7-10 days

cards.front: What are complications of acute tonsillitis?
cards.back: Chronic tonsillitis, peri-tonsillar abscess (Quinsy), para-pharyngeal abscess

cards.front: What defines RECURRENT tonsillitis?
cards.back: >7 episodes per year in <1 year, >5 episodes/year over 2 years, or >3 episodes/year over 3 years

cards.front: What percentage of deaths in SA children <5 years is due to ARI?
cards.back: 20% of deaths in under 5 years old

cards.front: What factors influence the incidence of ARI in children?
cards.back: Breastfeeding status, nutritional status, socio-economic status (poverty/overcrowding), HIV status, immunization status, parental smoking, parasitic infection, structural abnormalities, rainy and cold weather

cards.front: What organisms cause rhinitis (URTI)?
cards.back: Viral

cards.front: What organisms cause otitis media?
cards.back: Bacterial: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae

cards.front: What organisms cause tonsillitis and pharyngitis?
cards.back: Bacterial: Streptococcus

cards.front: What organisms cause sinusitis?
cards.back: Viral and Bacterial: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae

cards.front: What organisms cause epiglottitis?
cards.back: Bacterial: Haemophilus influenzae

cards.front: What organisms cause laryngotracheobronchitis?
cards.back: Viral: Para-influenza, measles

cards.front: What organisms cause tracheobronchitis?
cards.back: Viral

cards.front: What organisms cause bronchiolitis?
cards.back: Viral: RSV, Adenovirus, Parainfluenza

cards.front: What organisms cause bacterial pneumonia?
cards.back: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus, Haemophilus influenzae, Mycobacterium tuberculosis

cards.front: What organisms cause viral pneumonia?
cards.back: RSV, Adenovirus, Influenza, COVID-19

cards.front: What is the antibiotic treatment for Community Acquired Pneumonia in <3/12 or immunosuppressed child?
cards.back: Ampicillin/Gentamycin

cards.front: What is the antibiotic treatment for Community Acquired Pneumonia in >3/12 child?
cards.back: Ampicillin/Amoxil

cards.front: What antibiotic is used for CAP with aspiration/Staph concerns?
cards.back: Augmentin

cards.front: What is the 2nd line antibiotic for Hospital Acquired Pneumonia?
cards.back: Pip-Taz/Amikacin (with normal renal function and no CNS concerns)

cards.front: What is the 3rd line antibiotic for Hospital Acquired Pneumonia?
cards.back: Carbapenems

cards.front: What is stridor and when does it occur?
cards.back: High pitch sound. Starts during inspiration; as obstruction worsens, occurs during both inspiration and expiration. Indicates mostly extrathoracic airway obstruction.

cards.front: What does stridor associated with hoarseness indicate?
cards.back: Vocal cord involvement

cards.front: What does stridor that changes with head/neck position suggest?
cards.back: Supra-glottic aetiology

cards.front: What is wheeze and what does it indicate?
cards.back: Small or large airway disease/obstruction. Associated with hyperinflation and barrel-shaped chest. Initially more prominent in expiration.

cards.front: What is laryngomalacia?
cards.back: Most common congenital abnormality affecting the larynx; flaccidity of the supra-glottic region ('Floppy voice box')

cards.front: What are the clinical features of laryngomalacia?
cards.back: Inspiratory stridor from birth, disappears in prone position, absent/softer when infant is asleep

cards.front: How long does laryngomalacia stridor persist?
cards.back: Mild stridor improves with age and completely disappears by 1-2 years

cards.front: How is laryngomalacia diagnosed?
cards.back: Flexible pharyngo-laryngoscope

cards.front: What is the management for moderate to severe laryngomalacia with URTI?
cards.back: Admit, give humidified oxygen with adrenaline nebulization

cards.front: What is the most common cause of stridor in infancy?
cards.back: Croup

cards.front: What is the most common age range for croup?
cards.back: 6 months to 2 years

cards.front: What viral organisms most commonly cause croup?
cards.back: Parainfluenza (types 1, 2, and 3) and RSV. In developing countries, measles and herpes simplex still common causes of severe croup.

cards.front: What is stridor and when does it occur?
cards.back: High pitch sound. Starts during inspiration; as obstruction worsens, occurs during both inspiration and expiration. Indicates mostly extrathoracic airway obstruction.

cards.front: What does stridor associated with hoarseness indicate?
cards.back: Vocal cord involvement

cards.front: What does stridor that changes with head/neck position suggest?
cards.back: Supra-glottic aetiology

cards.front: What are the acute infectious causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Laryngotracheobronchitis (Croup), Epiglottitis, Bacterial tracheitis, Retropharyngeal abscess, Paratracheal gland enlargement

cards.front: What are the acute mechanical causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Foreign body, Tumor

cards.front: What are the acute allergic causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Angioneurotic oedema

cards.front: What are the acute traumatic causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Intubation injury, Thermal injury

cards.front: What are the chronic congenital causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Laryngomalacia, Tracheomalacia, Laryngeal cysts/clefts, Vascular rings

cards.front: What are the chronic infectious causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Laryngeal papillomatosis, Hypertrophy of tonsils and adenoids

cards.front: What are the chronic traumatic causes of upper airway obstruction?
cards.back: Subglottic stenosis

cards.front: What is wheeze and what does it indicate?
cards.back: Small or large airway disease/obstruction. Associated with hyperinflation and barrel-shaped chest. Initially more prominent in expiration.

cards.front: What are the causes of wheezing in children?
cards.back: Reactive airway disease (asthma), bronchial oedema (bronchiolitis), foreign body/aspiration, eosinophilia pneumonia, cardiac failure, cystic fibrosis, bronchiectasis

cards.front: What are chronic or recurrent causes of wheeze?
cards.back: Hypersensitivity reactions (Aspergillosis/CF), dynamic airway collapse (bronchomalacia), airway compression (vascular ring/lymph nodes), aspiration (GORD/TOF), failure to clear secretions (CF), intrinsic airway lesions (tumours/tracheal stenosis), congestive heart failure, parasites (Loeffler's Syndrome)

cards.front: When should asthma be considered in children?
cards.back: In ALL children with recurrent airway obstruction RELIEVED by bronchodilators

cards.front: What signs suggest asthma diagnosis?
cards.back: Family history of asthma in parents, signs of atopy

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Tracheal tug?
cards.back: Tracheal tug indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Supraclavicular recession?
cards.back: Supraclavicular recession indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Intercostal recession?
cards.back: Intercostal recession indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Subcostal recession?
cards.back: Subcostal recession indicates recession

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Nasal flaring?
cards.back: Nasal flaring indicates accessory muscles

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Abdominal breathing?
cards.back: Abdominal breathing indicates accessory muscles

cards.front: What is a sign of respiratory distress: Head bobbing?
cards.back: Head bobbing indicates accessory muscles - secondary to sternocleidomastoid contractions

cards.front: What general sign indicates increased work of breathing?
cards.back: Difficulty speaking or feeding, expiratory grunting, restlessness and agitation, drowsiness, cyanosis

cards.front: What is the most common cause of RTI in infants up to 2 years?
cards.back: Acute Viral Bronchiolitis

cards.front: What is the peak age for bronchiolitis?
cards.back: 3-4 months

cards.front: What children are at risk for severe bronchiolitis?
cards.back: Preterm infants, those with congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease

cards.front: What is the most common viral cause of bronchiolitis?
cards.back: RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus)

cards.front: What is the pathophysiology of bronchiolitis?
cards.back: Viral infection causes mucosal oedema, desquamation of bronchiole, leading to small airway obstruction, increased airway resistance and air trapping

cards.front: What is the WHO End TB strategy timeframe and its main goals?
cards.back: 2016 to 2035. Goals: 90% reduction in TB deaths and 80% reduction in TB incidence.

cards.front: What is the TB incidence in children compared to adults?
cards.back: TB incidence in children is approximately half that in adults.

cards.front: What proportion of infants (<12 months) progress to active TB disease after primary TB infection?
cards.back: 50% of infants progress to active TB disease after primary TB infection.

cards.front: What percentage of infants develop complicated TB (miliary TB and/or TB meningitis)?
cards.back: 10-20% of infants develop complicated TB (miliary TB and/or TB meningitis).

cards.front: Define adherence in TB management.
cards.back: The extent to which a person's behaviour corresponds with agreed recommendations from a healthcare worker for taking medication, following a diet and/or making lifestyle changes.

cards.front: What is the WHO definition of adolescent?
cards.back: A person aged 15 to 19 years.

cards.front: At what weight can children typically be treated with adult doses of TB medications?
cards.back: Children weighing >25kg (typically eight to ten years of age) can be treated with adult doses of TB medications.

cards.front: Define index patient in TB context.
cards.back: A person with infectious pulmonary TB who is newly diagnosed.

cards.front: Define latent TB infection.
cards.back: A state of immune response to stimulation by Mycobacterium tuberculosis antigens, indicated by a positive tuberculin skin test (TST) or interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) test, with no evidence of active TB disease.

cards.front: Define recent TB exposure.
cards.back: Contact with a person diagnosed with pulmonary TB in the last 12 months. The person may be from the same household, or be a close contact outside of the household setting, e.g., a care provider, colleague, teacher, family member or friend.

cards.front: Define significant TB exposure.
cards.back: Known exposure to a person (adult or adolescent) with pulmonary TB who shared the same enclosed space for one or more nights or for frequent or extended daytime periods during the three months before the index patient starting their TB treatment.

cards.front: Define TB contact.
cards.back: All people (family members and other individuals; regardless of age and HIV status) who have had a 'significant TB exposure' – shared the same enclosed space with a TB index patient for one or more nights or for frequent or extended daytime periods during three months before the start of current treatment.

cards.front: Define TB disease.
cards.back: Disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. TB can either be bacteriologically confirmed or clinically diagnosed.

cards.front: Define TB exposed infants.
cards.back: Infants born to mothers who were diagnosed with TB before or after the baby was born, or other documented close TB exposure in the infant (e.g. another caregiver, family member, day care provider).

cards.front: What does 3HP regimen stand for?
cards.back: Three months of weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine.

cards.front: What does 3RH regimen stand for?
cards.back: Three months of daily rifampicin plus isoniazid.

cards.front: What does 6H regimen stand for?
cards.back: Six months of daily isoniazid monotherapy.

cards.front: What does 12H regimen stand for?
cards.back: 12 months of daily isoniazid monotherapy.

cards.front: Define contact-tracing in TB management.
cards.back: The strategy by which individuals in contact with a case of confirmed or suspected pulmonary TB are screened for the presence of TB infection or disease, with the aim of offering either anti-tuberculosis preventive therapy or anti-tuberculosis chemotherapy.

cards.front: Define reverse contact-tracing.
cards.back: The strategy adopted when a child is the index case with active TB, and the potential source case (usually an adult or older child in the index case's home environment) is sought by symptom screening and/or chest radiography of household contacts.

cards.front: What are the key factors determining TB disease progression in children?
cards.back: Age of the child (<5 years of age), time since exposure/infection (±1 year), and immune status of the child.

cards.front: What is the typical size range of MTB bacilli?
cards.back: MTB bacilli range in length from 1-10 μm (micrometers).

cards.front: Why are children <10 years of age less likely to transmit TB?
cards.back: Children <10 years of age rarely develop lung cavities, and therefore are less likely to transmit the TB organism.

cards.front: What major pathogenic components does MTB have in its cell wall?
cards.back: Heat shock proteins (highly immunogenic), cord factor (glycolipid that induces granuloma), and LAM (lipoarabinomannan).

cards.front: What percentage of bacterial mass does LAM represent?
cards.back: LAM (lipoarabinomannan) represents 15% of the bacterial mass.

cards.front: What are the effects of LAM on immune response?
cards.back: LAM inhibits macrophage activation by interferon gamma, and induces macrophage to secrete IL-10 (suppresses TB-induced T cell proliferation) and TNF alpha (causing fever, weight loss and tissue damage).

cards.front: What is the Ghon focus and when does it develop?
cards.back: The Ghon focus is the initial inflammatory reaction site in the lung parenchyma where TB organism deposits. It develops within 2-3 weeks of infection and may resolve and calcify.

cards.front: What is the Ghon complex?
cards.back: The Ghon complex includes the Ghon focus in the lung parenchyma together with enlarged regional hilar lymph nodes. It represents a controlled and retained non-proliferating Mycobacterium.

cards.front: What are the three possible outcomes of TB infection in the primary phase?
cards.back: 1) Can be cleared by host immunity (primary infection). 2) Suppressed into inactive form (latent TB) with calcified scar visible on X-ray. 3) Spread (active TB).

cards.front: What is fibrocaseous TB?
cards.back: TB in which hydrolytic enzymes dissolve MTB in larger lesions surrounded by fibrous capsule. Fibrocaseous nodules contain viable mycobacterium and pose lifelong risk for reactivation.

cards.front: What is proliferative TB granuloma?
cards.back: Granuloma with effective killing of Mycobacterium due to proliferating lymphocytes, plasma cells, outer rim of fibrosis, and low bacillary load.

cards.front: What is exudative TB granuloma?
cards.back: Granuloma with higher bacillary load, caseating necrosis, and progression of infection if not treated. Also called caseating granuloma/fibrocaseous.

cards.front: List the constitutional symptoms of TB infection.
cards.back: Fever and night sweats, weight loss and failure to thrive, fatigue and anorexia.

cards.front: What number of symptom criteria is highly suggestive of TB diagnosis?
cards.back: 2 or more of the TB symptom criteria are highly suggestive of TB diagnosis.

cards.front: What is the symptom criterion for persistent cough in TB screening?
cards.back: Persistent or non-remitting cough/wheeze >2 weeks (unresponsive to appropriate antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia).

cards.front: What is the symptom criterion for weight loss in TB screening?
cards.back: Documented low weight or failure to thrive (FTT) for the past 3 months (especially if not responding to intervention).

cards.front: What are the clinical hypersensitivity phenomena occurring 4-12 weeks after primary TB infection?
cards.back: 1) TST conversion. 2) Erythema nodosum (raised red, painful macules on anterior lower legs). 3) Phlyctenular conjunctivitis (raised red nodule on limbus with conjunctival injection). 4) Poncet's polyarthritis.

cards.front: What is the clinical presentation of TB pulmonary disease with airway involvement?
cards.back: Atelectasis of the involved segment/lung, cough, enlarged regional nodes, and large airway (monophonic) wheezing that responds poorly to bronchodilators.

cards.front: What is the role of CD4 Th cells in TB infection?
cards.back: CD4 Th cells secrete interferon gamma, causing activation of macrophages into true killer cells, formation of reactive nitrogen intermediates, and epithelioid granuloma formation in clearance of mycobacterium.

cards.front: What is the role of CD8+ T suppressor cells in TB granuloma formation?
cards.back: CD8+ T suppressor cells kill macrophages that are infected, leading to caseating granuloma formation (delayed hypersensitivity reaction).

cards.front: Why can granulomas be paradoxical in TB control?
cards.back: While granulomas are acidic, anaerobic, extracellular environments that inhibit MTB growth, they can also cause tissue damage if they fail to contain the disease, leading to spread and further tissue destruction.

cards.front: What X-ray finding indicates controlled primary TB infection?
cards.back: Calcified scar in the lung parenchyma and/or hilar lymph nodes (Ghon focus/complex) indicating controlled and retained non-proliferating Mycobacterium.

cards.front: What is the TST (Tuberculin Skin Test) reading timeframe?
cards.back: The TST is read after 48-72 hours.

cards.front: What does a positive TST indicate?
cards.back: A positive TST indicates TB infection (not necessarily TB disease). It measures hypersensitivity to tuberculin purified protein derivative (PPD).

cards.front: What does a positive TST signify in children <5 years or HIV-infected children?
cards.back: In children <5 years or HIV-infected children, a positive TST indicates recent infection and is a risk factor for progression to active TB disease.

cards.front: What conditions can cause a false negative TST?
cards.back: HIV infection, malnutrition, severe viral infections (measles, chicken pox), cancer, immunosuppressive drugs (steroids), severe disseminated TB, and recent TB exposure (2-3 months conversion window period).

cards.front: How should a positive TST be interpreted in endemic countries after BCG vaccine?
cards.back: In endemic countries, a positive TST post BCG vaccine should be interpreted as indicative of TB infection, especially in very young and immunocompromised children.

cards.front: What is BCG vaccine derived from?
cards.back: BCG vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine derived from Mycobacterium bovis.

cards.front: When is BCG vaccine given and to whom?
cards.back: BCG vaccine is given to all neonates at birth via intradermal injection, except to symptomatic HIV-exposed neonates.

cards.front: What protection does BCG provide in TB-endemic countries?
cards.back: BCG provides children with a degree of protection against disseminated and severe forms of TB (TB meningitis and miliary TB).

cards.front: Should asymptomatic HIV-exposed babies receive BCG vaccine?
cards.back: Yes. Due to high TB burden, it is recommended that routine BCG vaccination be given at birth to asymptomatic HIV-exposed babies, but they must be monitored closely for BCG-related adverse events.

cards.front: List the danger signs requiring urgent hospital referral for TB.
cards.back: Severe respiratory distress (TB pneumonia), severe wheezing not responding to bronchodilators (severe airway compression), signs/symptoms of meningism and seizures (TBM), hepatosplenomegaly (disseminated TB), breathlessness and peripheral edema (pericardial effusion), severe pulmonary disease and malnutrition, distended abdomen with/without ascites (TB abdomen), and angulation of the spine gibbous (TB spine).

cards.front: Why is TB diagnosis more complex in HIV-infected children?
cards.back: TB disease diagnosis is indistinguishable from other HIV-related lung diseases, symptoms such as chronic cough, weight loss, and persistent fever are common to both, and TST is frequently negative.

cards.front: List the differential diagnoses of pulmonary TB in HIV-infected children.
cards.back: Bacterial pneumonia, viral pneumonia, fungal lung disease, pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia, pulmonary lymphoma, Kaposi's sarcoma, and lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP).

cards.front: What are the distinctive features of LIP (lymphoid interstitial pneumonia)?
cards.back: LIP is distinguished by clubbing, parotid enlargement, and generalized lymphadenopathy.

cards.front: In what scenarios can children be clinically diagnosed with TB without bacteriological confirmation?
cards.back: Chest X-ray abnormalities consistent with active TB, clinical picture only (empiric treatment), histological and biochemical tests suggestive of TB, or a combination of the above due to paucibacillary nature of TB in children.

cards.front: What is the most common radiological finding of TB in children?
cards.back: An enlarged hilar region (lateral CXR is useful to detect hilar adenopathy, doughnut sign).

cards.front: What radiological findings indicate airway compression by enlarged lymph glands?
cards.back: Ball valve effect (segmental/lobar hyperinflation) or complete airway obstruction (lobar collapse).

cards.front: What radiological appearance indicates miliary TB?
cards.back: Acute dissemination causes widespread fine millet-sized (1-2 mm) lesions with a 'millet seed' appearance.

cards.front: In what age group do pleural effusions occur in TB?
cards.back: Pleural effusions may occur in children older than 6 years.

cards.front: What radiological finding should not be misinterpreted as widened mediastinum in children?
cards.back: Widened mediastinum secondary to thymus (sail sign) should not be misinterpreted as mediastinal widening due to TB lymph nodes.

cards.front: Why is sputum collection challenging in young children with TB?
cards.back: In children <8 years, sputum is usually smear negative (paucibacillary), lung cavities are rare, and adequate sputum sample collection is difficult.

cards.front: How many sputum samples should be collected for optimal TB diagnosis?
cards.back: At least 2 sputum samples on 2 consecutive days should be collected, as the probability of a positive sputum increases with >1 sputum sample collection.

cards.front: What specimens should be sent for GXP and TB culture in extrapulmonary TB?
cards.back: Tissue specimens, site-specific fluids/secretions should be sent for GXP and TB culture.

cards.front: What is urine LAM testing?
cards.back: Detection of LAM (lipoarabinomannan) levels in the urine. LAM is a heat-stable glycolipid of the outer cell wall of Mycobacterium sp. released by metabolically active bacilli and can be used to detect TB.

cards.front: How does urine LAM sensitivity and specificity compare in TB diagnosis?
cards.back: Urine LAM sensitivity increases among children with severe immunosuppression, but specificity is lower than that of GXP (Gene Xpert).

cards.front: Who is at high risk for extrapulmonary TB?
cards.back: Children <2 years of age with immature cellular immune response are at high risk for extrapulmonary TB.

cards.front: When does extrapulmonary TB typically develop?
cards.back: EPTB occurs in the first few months after TB infection, or may develop years later (e.g., osteoarticular, renal) following reactivation of organism that was subclinically disseminated during primary infection.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of TB lymphadenitis?
cards.back: Enlarged cervical lymph nodes that are painless (unless secondarily infected), firm, and matted. They may become fluctuant prior to spontaneous drainage and sinus formation (scrofula).

cards.front: Where can TB lymphadenitis occur besides the neck?
cards.back: TB lymphadenitis may involve sites other than the neck.

cards.front: List other causes of enlarged lymph nodes that can mimic TB lymphadenitis.
cards.back: Persistent generalized lymphadenopathy (PGL), lymphoma, Kaposi sarcoma, sarcoidosis, and carcinomatous metastases.

cards.front: In what serous cavities can inflammatory tuberculous effusions occur?
cards.back: Pleural, pericardial, and peritoneal cavities.

cards.front: What is the typical age for TB pleural effusion?
cards.back: Usually in children >3 years, particularly in older children and adolescents.

cards.front: What are the clinical presentations of TB pleural effusion?
cards.back: Intermittent fever, unilateral chest pain, breathlessness, and rarely haemoptysis (which may occur in cavitatory disease).

cards.front: What are the physical signs of TB pleural effusion?
cards.back: Decreased chest movement and stony dullness on percussion.

cards.front: What chest X-ray findings indicate TB pleural effusion?
cards.back: Tracheal/mediastinal shift away from side of effusion, and unilateral or bilateral uniform white opacity with a concave upper border.

cards.front: What are the characteristics of pleural aspirate in TB?
cards.back: Exudate, straw colored, high protein, high WCC (white cell count), and raised adenosine deaminase (ADA).

cards.front: What additional investigation can be done on pleural fluid for TB?
cards.back: Pleural biopsy can be performed for Xpert (Gene Xpert) testing.

cards.front: What age group can develop TB bone and joint disease?
cards.back: Occasionally in infants, but more commonly in older children.

cards.front: What are the clinical symptoms of TB osteoarticular disease?
cards.back: Painful limbs and joints, and limp.

cards.front: What percentage of osteoarticular TB is spinal TB?
cards.back: 50% of osteoarticular TB is spinal TB (TB spine).

cards.front: What are the symptoms of spinal TB?
cards.back: Back pain lasting a few weeks, and acute spinal cord compression (lower limb weakness, bladder and bowel neurology) which is an emergency.

cards.front: What are the clinical signs of TB pericarditis?
cards.back: Congestive heart failure (CCF) or pericardial constriction, raised jugular venous pressure (JVP), palpable pulsus paradoxus, and pericardial friction rub.

cards.front: What are the complications of TB abdominal disease?
cards.back: Malnutrition with protein-losing enteropathy, abdominal distension (ascites or bowel distension), peritonitis, and biliary or lymphatic obstruction due to massive compressive abdominal lymph nodes.

cards.front: What is miliary TB?
cards.back: A complication of primary TB in young children resulting from widespread blood-borne dissemination of TB bacilli.

cards.front: What are the physical signs of miliary TB?
cards.back: Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, fever, tachypnea, cyanosis, respiratory distress, and other signs including papular lesions on skin or choroidal tubercles in retina.

cards.front: What is the chest X-ray appearance of miliary TB?
cards.back: Diffuse, uniformly distributed, small miliary shadows with a 'millet seed' appearance.

cards.front: What are the blood and biopsy findings in miliary TB?
cards.back: Full blood count shows pancytopenia, liver function tests are abnormal, and biopsy shows tubercles on histology.

cards.front: Where can MTB be isolated in miliary TB?
cards.back: MTB can be isolated from CSF (cerebrospinal fluid), bone marrow, and sputum in miliary TB.

cards.front: What is the typical history and symptoms of TB meningitis?
cards.back: TB contact, lethargy, headache (especially with early morning vomiting), irritability, drowsiness, convulsions, and weight loss.

cards.front: What are the physical signs of TB meningitis?
cards.back: Neck pain and resistance to neck flexion due to meningeal irritation (Kernig's sign), cranial nerve palsies, and altered level of consciousness.

cards.front: What are the CSF findings in TB meningitis?
cards.back: Raised protein, low glucose, low chloride, predominantly lymphocytes; gram stain is negative and AFB (acid-fast bacilli) are seldom found.

cards.front: What test can be performed on cerebrospinal fluid in TB meningitis?
cards.back: Xpert (Gene Xpert) can be performed on CSF for MTB detection and rifampicin susceptibility testing.

cards.front: Can the TST be negative in TB meningitis?
cards.back: Yes, TST can be negative in TB meningitis.

cards.front: Can chest X-ray be normal in TB meningitis?
cards.back: Yes, chest X-ray may be normal in children with TBM (TB meningitis).

cards.front: When should TB meningitis be considered in diagnosis?
cards.back: TB meningitis should always be considered in children diagnosed with meningitis and not responding to treatment.

cards.front: What are the complications of TB meningitis?
cards.back: Hydrocephalus (obstruction to CSF flow), SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone), hemi- or quadriplegia, convulsions, deafness, blindness, and neurodevelopmental delay.

cards.front: What are the modes of TB transmission from mother to baby?
cards.back: Transplacental spread through umbilical vein to fetal liver (in utero), aspiration or ingestion of infected amniotic fluid (in utero/intrapartum), and inhalation of droplet nuclei from close contacts - mother or other family members (postpartum).

cards.front: What are the risk factors for vertical TB transmission from mother to infant?
cards.back: HIV infection or TB in mother, lack of prenatal care, non-adherence to anti-TB treatment, infectiousness of the mother, and mother having multi-drug resistant TB.

cards.front: What proportion of babies born to mothers with active TB develop TB disease in the first year without TPT or BCG?
cards.back: 50% of babies born to mothers with active TB disease will develop TB disease in the first year if no TB preventive therapy (TPT) or BCG vaccination is given.

cards.front: Can TB be transmitted through breast milk?
cards.back: No. Breast milk does not transmit TB.

cards.front: What is the clinical presentation of congenital TB?
cards.back: Low birth weight (LBW), poor feeding, poor weight gain, hepatosplenomegaly (HSM), abdominal distension, rashes, lethargy, and respiratory symptoms.

cards.front: How is TB infection determined in congenital TB?
cards.back: TB infection is determined by TST (tuberculin skin test).

cards.front: When can active congenital TB disease become apparent?
cards.back: Active disease can become present at 2-4 weeks of age.

cards.front: What specimens should be collected to investigate congenital TB?
cards.back: Gastric washings for Xpert (Gene Xpert), microscopy and culture, and placenta for histopathology.

cards.front: When should TB treatment be commenced in congenital TB?
cards.back: TB treatment should be commenced while awaiting bacteriological confirmation as the disease progresses rapidly.

cards.front: What TB treatment regimen is used for congenital TB?
cards.back: Treatment is the same as for all children <8 years (Regimen 3A).

cards.front: How is response to TB treatment indicated in congenital TB?
cards.back: Response to treatment is indicated by increased appetite, weight gain, and radiological resolution.

cards.front: Is breastfeeding recommended for infants with TB exposure from the mother?
cards.back: Yes. Breastfeeding is recommended to infants irrespective of the TB status of the mother, as the risk of transmission through breast milk is negligible.

cards.front: Should BCG vaccine be given to neonates exposed to TB while screening for active disease?
cards.back: No. BCG should not be given to neonates exposed to TB while screening for active disease. Maternal infectiousness and drug susceptibility status should be carried out first.

cards.front: What investigations must be done before starting TB treatment in a child?
cards.back: Exclude HIV infection and assess all co-infected children for ART eligibility (antiretroviral therapy).

cards.front: What psychosocial support should be provided when a child is diagnosed with TB?
cards.back: Psychosocial support should be provided to both the child and the parents/guardians.

cards.front: Should referral for nutritional support be considered in TB management?
cards.back: Yes. Referral for nutritional support should be considered in children diagnosed with TB.

cards.front: What administrative steps should be taken when a child is diagnosed with TB?
cards.back: Complete the TB Register and make a note in the Road to Health Card.

cards.front: What contact investigation should be done when a child is diagnosed with TB?
cards.back: Ask about other children or adults in the household and screen them for TB.

cards.front: What are the four categories of paediatric TB management covered in APPENDIX I?
cards.back: A) Uncomplicated pulmonary tuberculosis. B) Complicated pulmonary tuberculosis. C) Disseminated (miliary) tuberculosis. D) Tuberculous meningitis (TBM).

cards.front: What documents were updated for TB management in children?
cards.back: Primary Healthcare STGs and EML 2020 edition (Section 17.4: Pulmonary tuberculosis) and Paediatric Hospital Level STGs and EML, 2017 edition (Chapter 10: Tuberculosis).

cards.front: What does development of TB in a child on ART indicate?
cards.back: It could indicate immune reconstitution (if within first 6 months of ART initiation), failure of the ART regimen, or new TB infection depending on exposure.

cards.front: How should TB be managed in a child developing TB while on ART?
cards.back: TB treatment should be started without delay and ART continued. The ART regimen should be reviewed and may need to be changed.

cards.front: What vitamin should be given to all children on both TB and ARV treatment?
cards.back: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) should be given to all children on TB and ARV treatment due to shared toxicities of the regimens.

cards.front: How is Ritonavir dosing adjusted when a child is on Lopinavir/Ritonavir?
cards.back: For each mL of lopinavir/ritonavir 80/20 mg/mL solution, add 0.75 mL of ritonavir 80 mg/mL solution. Ritonavir should be added at a dose of 0.75 times the volume of the lopinavir/ritonavir dose.

cards.front: What is Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS)?
cards.back: IRIS is observed in patients on TB treatment who start ART, characterized by worsening of disease after initial clinical improvement.

cards.front: When does IRIS typically occur and how long does it last?
cards.back: IRIS may occur during the first three months of ART, is generally self-limiting, and lasts 10-40 days.

cards.front: What are the clinical manifestations of IRIS in TB patients?
cards.back: Worsening disease after initial improvement, fever, increased size of lymph nodes, tuberculomas, and exacerbation of symptoms, signs, or X-ray manifestations.

cards.front: How should IRIS be managed in TB patients starting ART?
cards.back: TB treatment and ART should be continued. In some cases, the addition of corticosteroids might be useful.

cards.front: Can patients already on ART experience exacerbations when starting TB therapy?
cards.back: Yes. Patients already on ART may experience temporary exacerbations of symptoms, signs, or X-ray manifestations after beginning TB therapy.

cards.front: List all major TB drugs used in paediatric regimens.
cards.back: Isoniazid (H), rifampicin (R), rifapentine, pyrazinamide (Z), and ethambutol (E).

cards.front: What are the standard pediatric TB treatment regimens?
cards.back: TPT regimens: 3HP (3 months isoniazid + rifapentine weekly), 3RH (3 months rifampicin + isoniazid daily), 6H (6 months isoniazid daily), 12H (12 months isoniazid daily). TB disease treatment per regimen type (uncomplicated, complicated, miliary, or meningitis).

cards.front: What is Regimen 3A for TB disease treatment?
cards.back: Regimen 3A is used for children <8 years with uncomplicated and congenital TB disease (specific dosages and durations per national guidelines).

cards.front: What weight threshold determines adult TB drug dosing in children?
cards.back: Children weighing >25 kg (typically 8-10 years) can be treated with adult doses of TB medications.

cards.front: What is the standard duration of TB preventive therapy (TPT) regimens?
cards.back: 3HP: 3 months weekly. 3RH: 3 months daily. 6H: 6 months daily. 12H: 12 months daily.

cards.front: What is the dosing frequency for 3HP regimen?
cards.back: 3HP (three months of weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine) is administered weekly for 3 months.

cards.front: What is the dosing frequency for 3RH and 6H regimens?
cards.back: Both 3RH (3 months) and 6H (6 months) are administered daily.

cards.front: When is TB preventive therapy (TPT) indicated?
cards.back: TPT is indicated for TB contacts, TB-exposed infants, and children with latent TB infection to prevent progression to active TB disease.

cards.front: What are the TST cutoff values for TB diagnosis in different child populations?
cards.back: TST ≥5 mm in children <5 years, HIV-infected children, or those with evidence of TB exposure. TST ≥10 mm in children ≥5 years without risk factors. TST ≥15 mm in non-BCG vaccinated children without TB risk.

cards.front: What baseline investigations are needed before starting TB treatment?
cards.back: HIV testing, assess for ART eligibility in co-infected children, nutritional assessment, psychosocial evaluation, chest X-ray, and TB Register completion.

cards.front: What are adverse effects monitoring parameters during TB treatment?
cards.back: Monitor liver function tests, peripheral neuropathy, visual acuity (if on ethambutol), ototoxicity (if on streptomycin), and hypersensitivity reactions.

cards.front: How is treatment adherence assessed in pediatric TB?
cards.back: Regular clinic visits, pill counts, clinical response monitoring (appetite, weight gain, symptom resolution), and radiological improvement on follow-up chest X-ray.

cards.front: What is the treatment outcome definition for cured TB?
cards.back: Cured: Completed treatment with evidence of clinical and radiological improvement (no relapse for at least 12 months after treatment completion).

cards.front: What is treatment failure in pediatric TB?
cards.back: Treatment failure: Lack of clinical improvement or radiological deterioration despite adequate treatment and adherence for at least 4 months.

cards.front: What is default in TB treatment?
cards.back: Default: Loss to follow-up/interruption of treatment for more than 2 consecutive months after the start of treatment.

cards.front: What characterizes the intensive phase of TB treatment?
cards.back: Intensive phase: First 2 months of anti-TB drugs with maximum bactericidal activity to eliminate the majority of viable TB organisms.

cards.front: What characterizes the continuation phase of TB treatment?
cards.back: Continuation phase: Remaining treatment duration to prevent relapse and eliminate remaining bacilli. Duration varies (2-4 months typically) based on TB type and response.

cards.front: What is the typical total duration of uncomplicated pulmonary TB treatment in children?
cards.back: Typically 6 months total: 2 months intensive phase + 4 months continuation phase.

cards.front: What is the treatment duration for complicated pulmonary TB?
cards.back: Complicated pulmonary TB typically requires 8 months treatment: 2 months intensive + 6 months continuation phase.

cards.front: What is the treatment duration for miliary TB?
cards.back: Miliary TB typically requires 8 months treatment with potential for 2 months initial phase with 4 drugs depending on severity.

cards.front: What is the standard treatment duration for TB meningitis?
cards.back: TB meningitis typically requires 9-12 months treatment due to poor drug penetration into CSF and severity of disease.

cards.front: Are corticosteroids used in TB meningitis treatment?
cards.back: Yes. Corticosteroids (typically dexamethasone or prednisone) are used as adjunctive therapy in TB meningitis to reduce inflammation and prevent complications.

cards.front: What is the role of nutritional support in TB treatment?
cards.back: Nutritional support is essential as TB is associated with malnutrition, which impairs immune function and treatment response. Adequate nutrition promotes weight gain and recovery.

cards.front: What supplementation is recommended alongside TB drugs?
cards.back: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) is recommended to all children on TB and/or ARV treatment to prevent peripheral neuropathy from isoniazid toxicity.

cards.front: What drug interactions occur between TB drugs and ART?
cards.back: Rifampicin induces hepatic enzymes, reducing ART levels. Lopinavir/ritonavir levels are affected, requiring dose adjustments. Isoniazid may accumulate with certain PIs.

cards.front: What is the impact of TB treatment on TB transmission?
cards.back: Appropriate TB treatment renders a child non-infectious within 2 weeks of starting effective therapy, reducing transmission risk significantly.

cards.front: What infection control measures should be implemented during TB treatment?
cards.back: Respiratory isolation for symptomatic patients (first 2 weeks), good ventilation, use of masks for close contacts, and adherence to treatment for rapid infectivity reduction.

cards.front: How should treatment failures be managed?
cards.back: Treatment failures require: 1) Assess adherence 2) Investigate for drug malabsorption 3) Re-evaluate diagnosis 4) Perform drug susceptibility testing 5) Consider resistance and modify regimen accordingly.

cards.front: What are the key components of TB contact screening?
cards.back: Clinical assessment for TB symptoms, tuberculin skin test (TST) or IGRA, chest X-ray if symptomatic, and TB preventive therapy for eligible contacts.

cards.front: When should TB preventive therapy be started in contacts?
cards.back: TPT should be started after active TB disease has been excluded through clinical assessment, symptom screening, and radiography in TB contacts.

cards.front: What documents provide the updated guidance for pediatric TB management?
cards.back: WHO 2014 guidance for national TB programmes, National Guidelines on Treatment of TB infection 2023, and Southern African society for pediatric infectious diseases guidelines.

cards.front: What are the three clinical signs used to assess circulation in a child?
cards.back:
1. Cold hands
2. Capillary refill time (CRT) ≥3 seconds
3. Weak and fast pulse

cards.front: Define shock in a paediatric patient based on circulatory signs.
cards.back: All three signs present: cold hands AND capillary refill time ≥3 seconds AND weak fast pulse = SHOCK (Emergency)

cards.front: What is impaired circulation and how many circulatory signs must be present?
cards.back: 1 or 2 of the three signs present (cold hands, CRT ≥3 sec, weak fast pulse) = Impaired circulation (Priority)

cards.front: What is the critical capillary refill time cutoff for assessing shock?
cards.back: Capillary refill time ≥3 seconds (3 seconds or more)

cards.front: List the four main causes of shock in the paediatric setting.
cards.back:
1. Diarrhoea (fluid loss from circulation)
2. Blood loss (trauma, bleeding)
3. Capillary leak into the body
4. Septic shock

cards.front: What are the initial management steps for all shocked children?
cards.back:
1. Stop bleeding
2. Give oxygen
3. Keep warm
4. IV or IO access (take blood and DEFG)
5. Consider antibiotics for suspected sepsis

cards.front: What is the fluid bolus volume and infusion time for a child WITHOUT severe acute malnutrition in shock?
cards.back: 10 ml/kg bolus fast over 15 minutes using Balanced crystalloid (Ringers lactate OR Normal saline)

cards.front: What is the fluid bolus volume and infusion time for a child WITH severe acute malnutrition in shock?
cards.back: 10 ml/kg bolus over 30 minutes (cautious infusion) using Normal saline OR Ringers Lactate

cards.front: Why is fluid administration cautious in children with severe acute malnutrition?
cards.back: Reduced cardiac and renal reserve

cards.front: What fluids are appropriate for shock treatment in a paediatric patient?
cards.back: Balanced crystalloid solutions: Ringers lactate OR Normal saline

cards.front: What should be done during reassessment after fluid bolus administration?
cards.back:
Check for:
1. Signs of shock (cold hands, CRT, weak fast pulse)
2. Signs of heart failure (increasing pulse, tachypnoea, enlarged liver, gallop rhythm, crepitations)
3. Consciousness level and lethargy

cards.front: When should glucose be checked in a shocked child and what is the treatment threshold?
cards.back: Check for hypoglycaemia while bolus being given. Treat with 5 ml/kg 10% Dextrose if glucose <3 mmol/L

cards.front: What is the dosing for treating hypoglycaemia in a paediatric patient?
cards.back: 5 ml/kg of 10% Dextrose intravenously

cards.front: What are the three possible outcomes after giving the first fluid bolus to a shocked child?
cards.back:
1. Better (improved circulation)
2. No improvement (still shocked) - proceed to 2nd bolus
3. Worse (developing heart failure) - stop bolus

cards.front: What is the concern after administering a third fluid bolus and what is the next step?
cards.back: Concern: Replaced half circulation volume. Actions: Seek help, give glucose and antibiotics, consider intubation/ventilation/inotropes

cards.front: What volume of circulation is replaced after three fluid boluses of 10 ml/kg each?
cards.back: Approximately half the circulation volume (3 × 10 ml/kg = 30% of total body weight, approximately 50% of circulating volume)

cards.front: What is the fluid management plan for a child whose circulation improved after bolus therapy?
cards.back:
1. Stop bolus administration
2. If dehydration present: calculate % deficit × 10 × weight
3. If no dehydration: IV ½ DD @ 4 ml/kg/hr
4. Change to oral feeds/breast asap (when no shock)

cards.front: What is the maintenance IV fluid rate for a child without dehydration post-shock?
cards.back: ½ DD (half daily dextrose/dextrose-saline) @ 4 ml/kg/hr

cards.front: How should dehydration correction fluid volume be calculated?
cards.back: % deficit × 10 × weight (in kg)

cards.front: What does the acronym DEFG stand for in the context of emergency management?
cards.back:
D = Dextrose (glucose)
E = [specific to context]
F = [specific to context]
G = [specific to context]
(Checked during shock fluid bolus administration)

cards.front: What is the sequential bolus volume for the first shock bolus in a child without severe malnutrition?
cards.back: 10 ml/kg administered rapidly over 15 minutes

cards.front: What is done after the first rapid bolus if the child is still shocked?
cards.back: Reassess for signs of shock. If still shocked, administer second rapid bolus of 10 ml/kg over 15 minutes

cards.front: What is done after the second rapid bolus if the child is still shocked?
cards.back: Reassess for signs of shock. If still shocked, administer third rapid bolus of 10 ml/kg over 15 minutes. Beware - seek help

cards.front: What is the sequence and timing of fluid boluses for a child WITH severe acute malnutrition?
cards.back:
1st bolus: 10 ml/kg over 30 minutes - reassess if still shocked
2nd bolus: 10 ml/kg over 30 minutes - reassess if still shocked
3rd bolus: 10 ml/kg over 60 minutes - Beware (mark with asterisk)

cards.front: What is the infusion time difference between the second and third bolus in a malnourished child?
cards.back:
Second bolus: 30 minutes
Third bolus: 60 minutes (double the time)

cards.front: Which crystalloid fluids are used for rapid bolus administration in shock?
cards.back: Normal saline OR Ringers lactate (balanced crystalloid solutions)

cards.front: What must be taken during IV/IO access establishment in a shocked child?
cards.back: Blood for emergency laboratory investigations (DEFG parameters)

cards.front: What procedural steps must accompany IV/IO line insertion?
cards.back:
1. Fix cannula firmly
2. Immobilise extremity
3. Sterile technique
4. Safe needle disposal

cards.front: What is the indication for considering antibiotics in a shocked child?
cards.back: Suspected sepsis (septic shock)

cards.front: What specific antibiotic is recommended for septic shock?
cards.back: Ceftriaxone

cards.front: What blood product is indicated if haemoglobin is below what level in a shocked child?
cards.back: Transfuse if Hb <5 g/dL (transfuse fresh blood - TF)

cards.front: What should be done if a shocked child develops signs of heart failure during bolus administration?
cards.back: Stop bolus administration immediately. Give ½ DD @ 4 ml/hr. Seek help. Give glucose and antibiotics. Consider intubation, ventilation and inotropes

cards.front: What are the clinical signs of heart failure in a fluid-resuscitated child?
cards.back: Increasing pulse rate, tachypnoea, enlarged liver (hepatomegaly), gallop rhythm, crepitations at base of lungs

cards.front: How often should a shocked child be reassessed during and after fluid therapy?
cards.back: Reassess often and do not leave child unattended. Frequent reassessment is critical

cards.front: What parameters should be monitored during IV fluid administration in a shocked child?
cards.back:
1. Pulse rate
2. Respiratory rate
3. Signs of CCF (congestive cardiac failure)
4. Consciousness level

cards.front: Who is at higher risk for fluid overload complications during shock treatment?
cards.back: Malnourished children (particularly those with severe acute malnutrition)

cards.front: What are the complications of rapid intravenous fluid administration?
cards.back:
1. Fluid overload (especially in malnourished)
2. Pulmonary oedema (crepitations at lung bases)
3. Hepatomegaly
4. Heart failure

cards.front: What immediate action should be taken after shock improvement?
cards.back: Stop bolus administration

cards.front: When can oral feeds or breastfeeding be resumed after shock treatment?
cards.back: As soon as possible after shock has resolved (when child is no longer shocked)
