cards.back: Well controlled asthma: none of these in past week: daytime asthma symptoms more than twice per week, night-time awakening due to asthma, reliever (SABA) use more than twice per week, limitation of activity.

cards.front: Partly controlled asthma criteria?
cards.back: Partly controlled asthma: 1-2 of these in past week: daytime asthma symptoms more than twice per week, night-time awakening due to asthma, reliever (SABA) use more than twice per week, limitation of activity.

cards.front: Uncontrolled asthma criteria?
cards.back: Uncontrolled asthma: 3-4 of these in past week: daytime asthma symptoms more than twice per week, night-time awakening due to asthma, reliever (SABA) use more than twice per week, limitation of activity.

cards.front: What is metered-dose inhaler with spacer?
cards.back: MDI with spacer is the most common asthma pump. Spacers are simple holding chambers with or without a valve. Medication is suspended within the spacer and then breathed in, enhancing lung deposition.

cards.front: Simple spacer construction recommendation?
cards.back: A very effective spacer device can be constructed out of a 500 mL plastic cold-drink bottle. A hole large enough to take the mouthpiece of an MDI is cut (or burnt) in the bottom end of the bottle to form a simple low-cost non-valved spacer.

cards.front: Spacer use recommendation?
cards.back: A spacer is recommended for all children and adults with difficult-to-control asthma.

cards.front: Home nebulisers recommendation?
cards.back: Home nebulisers are not recommended for asthma management.

cards.front: Dry-powder inhalers suitability?
cards.back: Dry-powder inhalers (DPIs) are easy to use, but are only suitable for older children and adults because of the inspiratory flow required for their actuation. Measurements of peak inspiratory flow can be done with appropriate devices to assess suitability of DPIs.

cards.front: Asthma control definition?
cards.back: Asthma control is the extent to which the effects of asthma can be seen in a patient or have been reduced or removed by treatment. Evaluation includes symptom control (frequency of symptoms, reliever medication use, activity limitation over past week and month) and future risk (exacerbations, medication side-effects, lung function loss).

cards.front: Asthma control assessment interval?
cards.back: After initiation of treatment, it is essential to assess asthma control at every follow-up visit, no less than 6-monthly, and to adjust treatment accordingly.

cards.front: Viral infection prevention distance?
cards.back: Keep a distance from others (social distancing, approximately 1 metre).

cards.front: Hand washing duration for infection prevention?
cards.back: Wash your hands often for 20-30 seconds, always after coughing or sneezing.

cards.front: Mask wearing for asthma patients?
cards.back: Mask-wearing to protect people from coronavirus in public spaces is recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO) and endorsed for asthma patients. There is no evidence to suggest that mask-wearing is deleterious to people with asthma.

cards.front: ICS adherence in children percentage?
cards.back: Treatment adherence to asthma treatment in the paediatric population is poor, with studies reporting only one-third of children using ICS therapy appropriately.

cards.front: Adherence barrier types?
cards.back: Barriers to adherence may be intentional (driven by illness perceptions or medication beliefs, leading to patients and caregivers deliberately choosing not to follow treatment recommendations) or unintentional (related to family routines and socioeconomic factors).

cards.front: Asthma comorbidities improving control?
cards.back: Identification and treatment of associated comorbidities may improve asthma control: rhinitis or sinusitis, obesity, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD), allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA).

cards.front: Treatment review after initiation?
cards.back: Review asthma control in 4-6 weeks after starting treatment. Assess asthma control and step up or step down treatment, depending on level of control.

cards.front: ICS-formoterol 2021 guideline change?
cards.back: In children aged ≥6 years with moderate to severe persistent asthma, ICS-formoterol in a single inhaler is recommended as daily controller and reliever therapy compared with either higher-dose ICS as daily controller therapy and SABA for quick-relief or same-dose ICS-LABA as daily controller therapy and SABA for quick-relief therapy.

cards.front: ICS course during RTI children <6?
cards.back: In children <6 years of age with recurrent wheezing triggered by respiratory tract infections and no wheezing between infections, start a short course (7-10 days) of daily ICS at the onset of a respiratory tract infection with an as-needed short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA).

cards.front: ICS treatment 6-11 years guideline?
cards.back: In children 6-11 years of age, recommend as-needed SABA and a short course (7-10 days) of ICS at the start of an upper-respiratory tract infection or daily low-dose ICS.

cards.front: What are the eight long-term goals of asthma management?
cards.back:
The eight long-term goals include:
1. Achieve good symptom control
2. Maintain normal activity levels
3. Minimise future risk of asthma-related mortality
4. Reduce exacerbations
5. Maintain lung function and normal lung development
6. Minimise side-effects of treatment
7. Provide a written action plan
8. Consider the patient's own goals with regard to treatment

cards.front: What environmental factors should asthmatic children avoid?
cards.back: Asthmatic children should avoid environmental tobacco smoke and other indoor air pollutants, and exposure should be controlled for indoor allergens such as house-dust mites, pets, cockroach, and mould.

cards.front: Principles of asthma treatment step: device selection?
cards.back: One of the key principles of asthma treatment is to choose an appropriate device and delivery system suited to the patient's age and capability.

cards.front: Children <6 years Step 4 persistent asthma?
cards.back: Children <6 years with Step 4 persistent asthma should be referred to specialist for further assessment and management.

cards.front: Budesonide once-daily dosing approval?
cards.back: Budesonide is approved for once daily dosing in patients with mild illness.

cards.front: Age restriction for ciclesonide use?
cards.back: Ciclesonide is registered for children ≥12 years of age.

cards.front: Fluticasone to budesonide dose equivalence?
cards.back: Fluticasone propionate may be used at half the dose of budesonide equivalent dose due to its higher potency.

cards.front: HFA vs CFC preparations: dose conversion?
cards.back:
As chlorofluorocarbon (CFC) preparations are being taken off the market, hydrofluoroalkane (HFA) preparations are replacing them.
Medication inserts for HFA preparations should be carefully reviewed for the equivalent correct dosage, as the conversion factor may not be 1:1.

cards.front: Diagnostic red flags in wheezing: asymmetric findings?
cards.back: Clinical findings that suggest an alternative reason to wheeze (rather than asthma) include digital clubbing, growth faltering, and asymmetric wheeze.

cards.front: Allergen-specific IgE testing for asthma diagnosis?
cards.back: Presence of allergen-specific IgE (determined through skin-prick testing or ImmunoCAP) may support an asthma diagnosis by indicating atopic sensitisation.

cards.front: Pragmatic therapeutic trial with ICS: what does it confirm?
cards.back: A pragmatic therapeutic trial with correctly administered low-dose inhaled corticosteroid will confirm the presence of corticosteroid-responsive airway inflammation, supporting an asthma diagnosis.

cards.front: Asthma management cycle components?
cards.back:
The asthma management cycle has three essential components:
1. **Assess**: diagnosis, symptom control, risk factor assessment, medication technique, adherence
2. **Adjust**: medications, non-pharmacological strategies, treatment of modifiable risk factors
3. **Review**: medication effects and side-effects, response to treatment adjustments

cards.front: Distinguishing ICS response from natural resolution of preschool wheeze?
cards.back:
Symptoms resolving during ICS therapy may be due to either the natural history of the preschool wheezing disorder OR the effect of treatment.
To distinguish between these: withdraw treatment after initial improvement. Treatment should only be restarted if symptoms recur.

cards.front: What is primary hemostasis?
cards.back: Vascular contraction and platelet plug formation at site of injury

cards.front: What is secondary hemostasis?
cards.back: Coagulation factors respond in complex cascade to form fibrin strands to strengthen the platelet plug thus forming a fibrin clot

cards.front: List the components of an abnormal hemostatic system that can cause bleeding.
cards.back: Vascular, Platelets, von Willebrand factor, Coagulation pathway which includes fibrinolysis

cards.front: Is there a significant bleeding disorder? Is it hereditary or acquired?
cards.back: Key diagnostic questions to distinguish between inherited and acquired bleeding disorders

cards.front: Is the problem coagulation or platelets? Is there a factor deficiency or antibody?
cards.back: Key diagnostic questions to differentiate between platelet dysfunction and coagulation factor deficiency

cards.front: What should you always consider in a boy with bleeding?
cards.back: Always consider Hemophilia in a boy with bleeding

cards.front: If a child is ill, is the bleeding disorder more likely to be inherited or acquired?
cards.back: If child is ill – more likely acquired disorder

cards.front: Are petechiae rare or common in factor deficiency?
cards.back: Petechiae rare in factor deficiency

cards.front: Are multiple bleeding sites commoner in inherited or acquired bleeding disorders?
cards.back: Multiple bleeding sites commoner in acquired bleeding disorders

cards.front: If a single coagulation test is abnormal, is this more likely inherited or acquired?
cards.back: If a single test is abnormal more likely to be an inherited cause

cards.front: What physical finding indicates platelet disorder?
cards.back: Petechiae (wet vs dry), superficial ecchymosis, mucosal bleeding indicate platelet disorders (quantitative or qualitative)

cards.front: What physical finding indicates blood vessel disorder?
cards.back: Palpable purpura indicates blood vessel disorder (hypersensitivity, vasculitis)

cards.front: What physical findings indicate coagulation factor deficiency?
cards.back: Haemarthrosis, Deep ecchymosis ± Mucous membranes indicate Coagulation factors (VIII, IX) deficiency - Haemophilia A and B

cards.front: What conditions present with other signs of systemic involvement affecting platelets and coagulation?
cards.back: DIC, Leukemia, Liver disease with portal hypertension affect both platelets and coagulation factors

cards.front: Define petechiae.
cards.back: Red, nonblanching maculopapular lesions caused by intradermal capillary bleeding

cards.front: What is the size range of petechiae?
cards.back: Petechiae are pinpoint hemorrhages less than 2 mm in greatest diameter

cards.front: What is the size range of purpura?
cards.back: Purpura measures 2 mm to 1 cm

cards.front: What is the size range of ecchymoses?
cards.back: Ecchymoses are more than 1 cm

cards.front: What causes petechiae, purpura, and ecchymoses?
cards.back: Results from the extravasation of blood from the vasculature into the skin or mucous membranes

cards.front: What are the initial investigations for a bleeding child?
cards.back: Full blood count (FBC), Differential, Smear; INR/PT; APPT

cards.front: List the history components to assess in a bleeding child.
cards.back: Age/neonate, sex, single site/multiple sites, mucocutaneous/soft tissue, ill child/well child, family history, medication, immunisations/recent viral infections

cards.front: What physical examination findings should be assessed in a bleeding child?
cards.back: Ill child/well child, single site/multiple sites, mucocutaneous/soft tissue/joints, splenomegaly/organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, dysmorphism/skeletal abnormalities

cards.front: What physical findings indicate platelet disorders?
cards.back: Petechiae (wet vs dry), superficial ecchymosis, mucosal indicate quantitative or qualitative platelet disorders

cards.front: What physical finding indicates vascular disorder?
cards.back: Palpable purpura indicates blood vessel disorder (vasculitis, scurvy, hypersensitivity)

cards.front: What physical findings indicate coagulation factor deficiency (Haemophilia A and B)?
cards.back: Haemarthrosis, Deep ecchymosis ± Mucous membranes indicate Coagulation factors (VIII, IX) deficiency

cards.front: What conditions present with signs affecting both platelets and coagulation factors?
cards.back: DIC, Leukemia, Liver disease with portal hypertension (splenomegaly)

cards.front: Define petechiae with specific characteristics.
cards.back: Red, nonblanching maculopapular lesions caused by intradermal capillary bleeding

cards.front: What is the maximum diameter of petechiae?
cards.back: Pinpoint hemorrhages less than 2 mm in greatest diameter

cards.front: What size range defines purpura?
cards.back: 2 mm to 1 cm in diameter

cards.front: What size defines ecchymoses?
cards.back: More than 1 cm in diameter

cards.front: What causes petechiae, purpura, and ecchymoses?
cards.back: Extravasation of blood from the vasculature into the skin or mucous membranes

cards.front: Define thrombocytopenia.
cards.back: Platelet count < 150 x 10⁹/L

cards.front: Describe platelet development pathway.
cards.back: Hematopoietic stem cell differentiates to megakaryoblast → megakaryocyte (bone marrow) under influence of Thrombopoietin

cards.front: How many platelets does each megakaryocyte produce?
cards.back: Each megakaryocyte produces about 4000 platelets

cards.front: What is the half-life of platelets?
cards.back: 7-10 days

cards.front: What platelet count level impairs primary hemostasis?
cards.back: Platelet count < 75 x 10⁹/L impairs primary hemostasis, seen after major surgery or trauma

cards.front: At what platelet count does spontaneous bleeding mostly occur?
cards.back: Platelet count < 50 x 10⁹/L; spontaneous bleeding, mostly seen in skin (petechiae, purpura)

cards.front: At what platelet count is noticeable hemorrhage seen in skin and mucosa?
cards.back: Platelet count < 20 x 10⁹/L; noticeable hemorrhage seen in skin and mucosa (epistaxis, gingival bleeding)

cards.front: At what platelet count is life-threatening hemorrhage possible?
cards.back: Platelet count < 10 x 10⁹/L; possible life-threatening hemorrhage and CNS complications (acute GI hemorrhage, intracranial hemorrhage)

cards.front: What are the three main mechanisms of thrombocytopenia?
cards.back: Increased platelet destruction (immune, nonimmune), Decreased platelet production (congenital, acquired), Sequestration of platelets

cards.front: List immune causes of increased platelet destruction.
cards.back: ITP (Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura) - most common cause in children, Neonatal Allo-immune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT), Neonatal Autoimmune Thrombocytopenia, Autoimmune/Collagen vascular disorders, Viruses (e.g. HIV), Drug Induced

cards.front: What is the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in children?
cards.back: ITP (Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura)

cards.front: List non-immune causes of increased platelet destruction.
cards.back: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC), Hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS), Thrombotic thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP), Sepsis

cards.front: What bone marrow conditions cause thrombocytopenia through decreased platelet production?
cards.back: Primary malignancy (e.g. leukemia), Bone marrow failure (e.g. aplastic anemia - acquired/genetic disorder like Fanconi's anemia), Bone marrow infiltration (e.g. neuroblastoma, storage disorders, infections), Infections (e.g. HIV, Parvo, CMV, TB), Cytotoxic drugs, radiation

cards.front: What congenital defects cause selective ineffective thrombopoiesis?
cards.back: TAR syndrome, WAS (Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome), CAMT (Congenital Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia), Bernard-Soulier syndrome

cards.front: What viral infections cause selective ineffective thrombopoiesis?
cards.back: EBV, CMV, HIV

cards.front: Define hypersplenism as a cause of platelet sequestration.
cards.back: Sequestration of platelets in enlarged spleen due to infection, congestion, red cell disorders (hemolytic anemias), or infiltrative disorders

cards.front: What is Kasabach-Merritt Syndrome?
cards.back: Massive hemangioma causing platelet sequestration and thrombocytopenia

cards.front: What causes dilutional thrombocytopenia?
cards.back: Recent massive blood transfusion

cards.front: What is the annual incidence of ITP in children?
cards.back: 5 per 100,000 children annually

cards.front: How does ITP typically present in children?
cards.back: Sudden onset of petechiae/bruising/mucosal bleed in previously healthy child with absent abnormal physical findings; often preceded by viral infection or recent immunisation

cards.front: What is the pathogenesis of ITP?
cards.back: Antiplatelet antibody (autoantibody) binds to platelet surface with destruction enhanced by Fc-receptor mediated phagocytosis in the spleen and liver

cards.front: What immunological abnormalities are noted in ITP?
cards.back: Abnormalities in T-cells, B-cells and cytokines; exact mechanism of immune dysfunction not fully known

cards.front: What proportion of HIV-positive patients develop immune thrombocytopenic purpura?
cards.back: 30% of HIV-positive patients develop immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)

cards.front: List causes of increased platelet loss/destruction in HIV-positive patients.
cards.back: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (30%), Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), Hemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS), Hypersplenism (infection, hemophagocytosis), Drugs (interferon, saquinavir)

cards.front: List drugs that cause decreased platelet production in HIV patients.
cards.back: Co-trimoxazole, ganciclovir, fluconazole, rifabutin, clarithromycin, didanosine

cards.front: List infections that cause decreased platelet production in HIV patients.
cards.back: HIV, CMV, MTB (mycobacterium tuberculosis), MAC (mycobacterium avium complex), Histoplasmosis

cards.front: What malignancies cause decreased platelet production in HIV patients?
cards.back: Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma, Hodgkin's disease

cards.front: List deficiencies that cause decreased platelet production in HIV patients.
cards.back: Vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency

cards.front: What is the management approach for platelet count 20-50 x 10⁹/L on immune basis?
cards.back: Observe

cards.front: What is the management for platelet count > 20 x 10⁹/L with no mucosal bleed?
cards.back: Observe and educate

cards.front: What is the treatment for platelet count > 20 x 10⁹/L with mucosal bleed?
cards.back: Treat (with IVIG or steroids)

cards.front: What is the treatment for platelet count < 20 x 10⁹/L or mucosal bleed?
cards.back: IVIG at 1 gram/kg over 6 hours OR Steroids (short course); NO PLATELETS

cards.front: What is the IVIG dose for immune thrombocytopenia?
cards.back: 1 gram/kg over 6 hours

cards.front: In HIV-positive patients with thrombocytopenia, what is the first management step?
cards.back: Initiate HAART (Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy)

cards.front: In HIV-positive patients with thrombocytopenia, what should be treated?
cards.back: Treat underlying infection

cards.front: In HIV-positive patients with thrombocytopenia, what should be removed?
cards.back: Remove offending drugs

cards.front: Why should steroids be used cautiously in HIV patients with thrombocytopenia?
cards.back: Steroids may aggravate underlying TB, viral or fungal infection and underlying immune deficiency

cards.front: What should NOT be done for epistaxis?
cards.back: Put the head between the knees, Make the child lie down, Stuff gauze into the nose, Let the child blow the nose once bleeding stops, Panic (60% of population have nosebleed at least once in lifetime)

cards.front: What SHOULD be done for epistaxis?
cards.back: Remain calm and keep patient calm, Make the child sit up, Lean the head forward, Pinch the soft part of the nostril firmly, Press toward the face for 15 min, Repeat if necessary

cards.front: How long should pressure be applied to the nostril for epistaxis?
cards.back: 15 minutes

cards.front: Define NAIT (Neonatal Allo-immune Thrombocytopenia).
cards.back: Thrombocytopenia in neonates born to mothers with normal platelet count, analogous to Rh hemolytic disease

cards.front: When does NAIT typically present?
cards.back: Neonate born with profound thrombocytopenia or develops it within hours

cards.front: What clinical manifestations does NAIT cause?
cards.back: Petechiae or bruising; can bleed into viscera

cards.front: What is the most serious complication of NAIT?
cards.back: Intracranial hemorrhage (ICH), occurring in 8-22% of cases; can occur in utero

cards.front: What is the duration of NAIT?
cards.back: Lasts 2-3 weeks; resolves spontaneously

cards.front: List inherited coagulation disorders.
cards.back: Hemophilia A, Hemophilia B, Von-Willebrand disease, Deficiency of Factors II, V, VII, X, XI, XII or XIII, Dys-, hypo- or afibrinogenemia, Other rare disorders

cards.front: List acquired coagulation disorders.
cards.back: Vitamin K deficiency, Liver disease, DIC, Massive transfusion syndrome, Dys- or hypo-fibrinogenemia, Disorders associated with malignancy, Other rare disorders

cards.front: What PT/INR result indicates Vitamin K deficiency, liver disease, Warfarin, or Factor VII deficiency?
cards.back: ↑ PT/INR with Normal APTT indicates Vitamin K deficiency, Liver disease, Warfarin, or Factor VII deficiency

cards.front: What pathway does PT/INR test measure?
cards.back: Extrinsic pathway (PT/INR based factors)

cards.front: What APTT result indicates Factor VIII, Factor IX, Factor XI/XII deficiency, or Heparin?
cards.back: Normal PT/INR with ↑ APTT indicates Factor VIII deficiency (Hemophilia A), Factor IX deficiency (Hemophilia B), Factor XI or XII deficiency, or Heparin

cards.front: What pathway does APTT test measure?
cards.back: Intrinsic pathway (APPT based factors)

cards.front: What coagulation test pattern indicates Factor II, V, X deficiency, Vitamin K deficiency, Liver disease, or DIC?
cards.back: ↑ PT/INR AND ↑ APPT indicates Factor II, V, or X deficiency; Vitamin K deficiency; Liver disease; Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)

cards.front: What pathway do both PT/INR and APPT test together indicate?
cards.back: Common pathway (both extrinsic and intrinsic pathway factors)

cards.front: What does normal PT/INR and APPT with normal platelet count indicate?
cards.back: All tests normal including platelet count - find another cause for the bleeding

cards.front: What factors does Vitamin K activate?
cards.back: Vitamin K responsible for ϒ-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, and X (activation of these factors)

cards.front: Define Hemorrhagic Disease of the Newborn.
cards.back: Due to Vitamin K deficiency in newborn

cards.front: When does Hemorrhagic Disease of the Newborn classically present?
cards.back: Between day 2 and 3 after birth

cards.front: What are typical bleeding sites in Hemorrhagic Disease of the Newborn?
cards.back: Umbilicus, GIT, nose, skin, post-circumcision

cards.front: What is the most severe complication of Hemorrhagic Disease of the Newborn?
cards.back: Intracranial bleeding

cards.front: What prophylaxis is recommended for all newborns?
cards.back: All newborns to receive Vitamin K prophylaxis prior to discharge home

cards.front: What hemostatic disorders may present with normal coagulation screen and full blood count?
cards.back: Platelet function abnormalities (acquired: aspirin, uremia; inherited), Mild von Willebrand disease, Factor XIII deficiency, Collagen disorders, Vitamin C deficiency, Mild factor deficiencies

cards.front: List acquired causes of platelet function abnormalities.
cards.back: Drugs like aspirin, Uremia

cards.front: What additional test should be done if hemostasis is abnormal despite normal screening tests?
cards.back: Do bleeding time and Refer to hematologist

cards.front: What is the first step while waiting for diagnosis in a bleeding child?
cards.back: Control bleeding - Firm pressure/elevate limb

cards.front: What should be given if coagulation defect is suspected?
cards.back: Give FDP (Fresh Frozen Plasma) or FFP

cards.front: What should be given if thrombocytopenia is suspected?
cards.back: Give platelets

cards.front: What procedures should be avoided in a child with bleeding disorders?
cards.back: Avoid IMI (intramuscular injection), LP (lumbar puncture), cut-down, extractions, sutures, jugular or femoral venepunctures

cards.front: What medication should be avoided in a child with bleeding disorders?
cards.back: Avoid aspirin

cards.front: What is the management principle for replacement therapy in bleeding disorders?
cards.back: Prompt replacement therapy

cards.front: What is the ETAT protocol?
cards.back: ETAT is the emergency assessment protocol for Coma and Convulsions.

cards.front: What are the emergency triage signs (A-B-C-D)?
cards.back: A=Airway, B=Breathing, C=Circulation/Coma/Convulsion, D=Dehydration (severe)

cards.front: What does 'A' represent in ABCD triage?
cards.back: Airway

cards.front: What does 'B' represent in ABCD triage?
cards.back: Breathing

cards.front: What does 'C' represent in ABCD triage?
cards.back: Circulation, Coma, Convulsion

cards.front: What does 'D' represent in ABCD triage?
cards.back: Dehydration (severe)

cards.front: List the four main objectives for managing coma and convulsions.
cards.back: 1. Assess consciousness using the AVPU score; 2. Identify the child in coma; 3. Identify the child having convulsions; 4. Treat the child with coma and convulsions

cards.front: What does 'A' represent in the AVPU consciousness score?
cards.back: Alert

cards.front: What does 'V' represent in the AVPU consciousness score?
cards.back: Is the child responding to Voice?

cards.front: What does 'P' represent in the AVPU consciousness score?
cards.back: Is the child responding only to Pain?

cards.front: What does 'U' represent in the AVPU consciousness score?
cards.back: The child who is Unresponsive to voice (or being shaken) AND to pain is Unconscious

cards.front: What components of AVPU indicate coma?
cards.back: P and U in AVPU = Coma (responding only to Pain or Unresponsive)

cards.front: Define coma using the AVPU score.
cards.back: Coma = P (responding only to Pain) or U (Unresponsive) on AVPU scale

cards.front: List the clinical signs of a child actively convulsing.
cards.back: 1. Sudden loss of consciousness; 2. Uncontrolled jerky movements of the limbs and/or face; 3. Stiffening of the arms and legs; 4. Uncontrolled movements of the arms and legs; 5. Twitching of the face, hands or feet; 6. Abnormal movements of the eyes; 7. Brief apnoeas

cards.front: What is the first sign that indicates a child may be convulsing?
cards.back: Sudden loss of consciousness

cards.front: Name all involuntary movement features of convulsions.
cards.back: Uncontrolled jerky movements of limbs/face, stiffening of arms/legs, uncontrolled movements of arms/legs, twitching of face/hands/feet, abnormal eye movements

cards.front: What respiratory sign may occur during convulsions?
cards.back: Brief apnoeas (temporary cessation of breathing)

cards.front: What is the first step in treating a child with coma?
cards.back: Manage the airway and breathing

cards.front: Describe the correct positioning for airway management in an infant with coma.
cards.back: Neutral position

cards.front: Describe the correct positioning for airway management in an older child with coma.
cards.back: Sniffing position

cards.front: What is the jaw thrust maneuver and when is it used?
cards.back: Jaw thrust is an airway management technique used in coma/convulsion to open airway without flexing the spine

cards.front: When should the cervical spine be protected during coma management?
cards.back: If trauma is suspected

cards.front: How should suction be performed in an unconscious child?
cards.back: Gently (gentle suctioning to avoid causing further harm)

cards.front: What should NOT be placed in the mouth of a convulsing child?
cards.back: Nothing should be placed into the mouth

cards.front: How should oxygen be administered to a child with coma?
cards.back: Via face mask

cards.front: What metabolic complication must be checked in every coma/convulsion case?
cards.back: Blood glucose (must not ever forget glucose — D-E-F-G: Don't Ever Forget Glucose)

cards.front: What is hypoglycaemia defined as in paediatrics?
cards.back: Low blood glucose < 3 mmol/L

cards.front: What is the alternative unit for the hypoglycemia threshold?
cards.back: 55 mg/dL (equivalent to < 3 mmol/L)

cards.front: What is the treatment dose of IV glucose for hypoglycaemia?
cards.back: 5 ml/kg of 10% glucose solution, given rapidly by IV injection

cards.front: When should IV glucose be given in coma management?
cards.back: If blood glucose is low (< 3 mmol/L or 55 mg/dL) or if Dextrostix is not available

cards.front: What does 'D-E-F-G' mean in resuscitation?
cards.back: Don't Ever Forget Glucose (mnemonic for checking blood glucose as part of coma/convulsion assessment)

cards.front: What are the first steps when establishing IV access in a comatose child?
cards.back: Insert IV line and draw blood rapidly for emergency laboratory investigations

cards.front: What recovery position should be used after seizure management?
cards.back: Recovery position (patient placed on side to prevent aspiration)

cards.front: What is the IV glucose dosing protocol per Chart 9?
cards.back: 5 ml/kg of 10% glucose solution, given rapidly by IV injection. Insert IV line and draw blood rapidly for emergency investigations. Check blood glucose — if low (< 3 mmol/L or 55 mg/dL) or if Dextrostix unavailable, give the glucose dose.

cards.front: What is the blood glucose threshold for IV glucose administration?
cards.back: < 3 mmol/L (55 mg/dL)

cards.front: List all airway and breathing management steps for convulsions.
cards.back: 1. Position the child; 2. Suction gently; 3. Do not place anything into the mouth; 4. Give oxygen via face mask

cards.front: What is the main goal after airway management in convulsions?
cards.back: Stop the seizure using anticonvulsant medication

cards.front: Define status epilepticus.
cards.back: A seizure that lasts for > 5 minutes (more than 5 minutes)

cards.front: List the major complications of coma and convulsions.
cards.back: 1. Status epilepticus (seizure lasting > 5 minutes); 2. Brain swelling; 3. Brain damage after cell death; 4. Choking and aspiration of vomitus; 5. Death

cards.front: What is the consequence of prolonged seizure activity on the brain?
cards.back: Brain swelling and brain damage after cell death

cards.front: What aspiration risks exist in convulsing children?
cards.back: Choking and aspiration of vomitus

cards.front: What class of drug is used first-line for seizure control?
cards.back: Benzodiazepines (e.g., Lorazepam) — short-acting anticonvulsants

cards.front: Name the first-line anticonvulsant benzodiazepine.
cards.back: Lorazepam

cards.front: What is the second-choice benzodiazepine if Lorazepam unavailable?
cards.back: Midazolam

cards.front: What is the third-choice benzodiazepine if Lorazepam and Midazolam unavailable?
cards.back: Diazepam

cards.front: What is the preferred route for benzodiazepine administration?
cards.back: IV (intravenous) route

cards.front: If IV access is not available, what alternative routes can be used for Midazolam or Lorazepam?
cards.back: IMI (intramuscular) or buccal routes (but NOT for Diazepam; Diazepam uses PR route — rectal)

cards.front: If seizure continues after benzodiazepines, what is the next drug of choice?
cards.back: Phenobarbitone (phenobarbital)

cards.front: What is the drug of choice for neonates (< 4 weeks old) with convulsions?
cards.back: Phenobarbitone (phenobarbital)

cards.front: Why are benzodiazepines avoided in neonates without ventilatory support?
cards.back: Risk of apnoea (respiratory depression) is high in neonates

cards.front: What is the drug of first choice for seizure control when IV Phenobarbitone is available?
cards.back: Phenobarbitone (phenobarbital) for continuing seizures after benzodiazepines

cards.front: What is the alternative if IV Phenobarbitone is not available?
cards.back: Phenytoin (but MUST be given as an infusion over 30 minutes)

cards.front: How should Phenytoin be administered?
cards.back: As an infusion over 30 minutes (minimum infusion time)

cards.front: What cardiac complications require stopping Phenytoin infusion?
cards.back: If any arrhythmia, bradycardia, or hypotension occurs — STOP Phenytoin

cards.front: What is the management if bradycardia or hypotension occurs during Phenytoin infusion?
cards.back: Stop Phenytoin immediately; once cardiac status settles, resume infusion over 1 hour (slower rate)

cards.front: What does 'A-B-C' stand for in post-seizure assessment?
cards.back: A = Airway, B = Breathing, C = Circulation (recheck vital parameters)

cards.front: List all post-seizure management steps.
cards.back: 1. Recheck A-B-C (Airway, Breathing, Circulation); 2. Monitor for further seizures; 3. Treat for infection; 4. Recheck blood glucose; 5. Provide after-care

cards.front: Why must blood glucose be rechecked after seizure control?
cards.back: To ensure hypoglycaemia has not recurred and to guide ongoing management

cards.front: What investigations should be considered after seizure control?
cards.back: Emergency laboratory investigations (drawn at time of IV access for infection workup)

cards.front: What is the main treatment principle for seizures caused by infection?
cards.back: Treat for infection (antibiotics or antimalarials as appropriate)

cards.front: According to the course objectives, what must you be able to do regarding coma assessment?
cards.back: Assess consciousness using the AVPU coma score

cards.front: According to the course objectives, what must you identify regarding coma?
cards.back: Identify the child in coma (Unconscious)

cards.front: According to the course objectives, what must you identify regarding convulsions?
cards.back: Identify the child having convulsions

cards.front: According to the course objectives, what must you be able to treat?
cards.back: Treat the child with coma and convulsions

cards.front: What is the key mnemonic for remembering to check blood glucose?
cards.back: D-E-F-G: Don't Ever Forget Glucose

cards.front: Summarize the emergency triage assessment (A-B-C-D).
cards.back: After assessing Airway (A) and Breathing (B), assess Circulation (C) for Circulation status, Coma, and Convulsion. Also assess for Dehydration (D) severity.

cards.front: What is the relationship between consciousness level (AVPU) and seizure management priorities?
cards.back: Assess consciousness first using AVPU; if P or U (coma), manage airway/breathing; if convulsing, stop seizure with anticonvulsants after airway management

cards.front: What is DEHYDRATION in paediatric emergency care?
cards.back: A state of fluid loss from body tissues requiring assessment and fluid replacement in children with diarrhea and other causes of fluid loss. Initial assessment screens for shock vs non-shock dehydration.

cards.front: What are the Priority Signs 3TPR-MOB in ETAT assessment?
cards.back: Tiny baby, Temperature, Trauma, Pallor, Poisoning, Pain (severe), Respiratory, Restless, Referral (urgent), Malnutrition, Oedema, Burns

cards.front: When should assessment for severe dehydration be performed in a child with diarrhea?
cards.back: After assessment for Circulation/Coma/Convulsions (shock signs). If no diarrhea present, proceed to priority assessment (3TPR-MOB) instead.

cards.front: What is the definition of diarrheal illness with dehydration?
cards.back: Abnormal fluid loss through the gastrointestinal tract requiring classification into non-severe, severe, or shock categories based on clinical assessment.

cards.front: What is the basic approach to treatment decisions in severe dehydration?
cards.back: Assess for shock. Determine if oral fluids will be tolerated or IV fluids needed. Choose appropriate fluid type (ORS vs dextrose water vs dextrose saline). Determine infusion rate and duration based on severity and malnutrition status.

cards.front: What does diarrheal loss equivalent mean vs plasma equivalent loss?
cards.back: Diarrheal loss is stool loss (hypotonic). Plasma equivalent loss refers to other fluid losses (vomiting, NG drainage, bleeding) which may be more electrolyte-rich and require different replacement considerations than pure diarrheal loss.

cards.front: What are the key considerations in severe malnutrition and fluid management?
cards.back: Children with severe malnutrition have precarious metabolic and circulatory status far more than other children. Fluid and sodium handling differs: slower rehydration rates are safer (10ml/kg/hr over 8 hours vs standard 10ml/kg/hr over 4 hours). Glucose status must be monitored and managed as hypoglycemia is a major risk.

cards.front: When is shock assessment required in dehydration management?
cards.back: Assess for shock (ETAT criteria) before initiating rehydration plans. Look for cold hands, capillary refill time 3 seconds or more, and weak/fast pulse. Shock requires fluid bolus therapy before continuing standard rehydration.

cards.front: What special monitoring is needed during fluid bolus administration?
cards.back: While fluid bolus is being administered: treat hypoglycemia with 5 ml/kg 10% dextrose if glucose less than 3 mmol/L; give IV antibiotics if not already given; measure pulse and breathing rate frequently; observe for signs of congestive cardiac failure.

cards.front: What are the clinical signs of severe dehydration used for assessment?
cards.back: Three key signs: (1) Lethargy or altered consciousness, (2) Sunken eyes, (3) Skin pinch goes back very slowly (longer than 2 seconds). If diarrhea + 2 of these signs present (but child not shocked), classify as severe dehydration.

cards.front: What are additional assessment criteria for severe dehydration?
cards.back: In addition to the 3 main signs, also assess for: (1) Severe malnutrition (visible severe wasting and/or oedema both feet), (2) Need to check and treat glucose levels if severe malnutrition present

cards.front: What is the purpose of the Nasogastric (NG) tube in dehydration management?
cards.back: NG tubes allow administration of oral fluids (ORS) when children cannot drink sufficient volumes orally. Provides non-invasive fluid access when IV access is difficult, allowing continued oral route for fluid replacement.

cards.front: What is Plan A for oral rehydration in severe dehydration?
cards.back: Attempt Oral Rehydration with ORS 5 ml/kg via nasogastric tube OR ORAL every 15 minutes for 2 hours and reassess. If improving, continue for another 2 hours. If child vomits everything and shows no improvement after 2 hours, proceed to Plan B (IV fluids).

cards.front: What is Plan B for IV fluid therapy when oral rehydration fails?
cards.back: Give IV dextrose-dextrose 10 ml/kg for 4 hours and reassess every 1-2 hours. Once dehydration has resolved, progressively slow down the IV rate while continuing to give oral fluids. Begin oral ORS as soon as the child is able to drink at 5 ml/kg/hr.

cards.front: What is the maintenance phase after rehydration in severe dehydration?
cards.back: Once fully hydrated and on low IV rate, proceed to preventative ORS at 10 ml/kg after each stool and observe for 6 hours to ensure hydration is maintained. Can transition to maintenance fluids by oral route if possible, or IV if not tolerated.

cards.front: What alternative IV access methods exist when peripheral IV line cannot be established?
cards.back: If unable to get peripheral IV access: (1) Intraosseous (IO) fluids: DD 10 ml/kg/hr x 4 hours, (2) Nasogastric fluids: ORS 20 ml/kg/hr x 6 hours (best given as continuous infusion or as 5 ml/kg every 15 minutes, NOT as 20 ml/kg bolus every hour)

cards.front: What is the difference between Standard and Rapid Rehydration in severe dehydration without malnutrition?
cards.back: Standard Rehydration: 10 ml/kg/hr IV over 4 hours (replaces large fluid loss fairly rapidly). Rapid Rehydration without severe malnutrition: 20 ml/kg/hr DD IV over 4 hours. With severe malnutrition: use 10 ml/kg/hr over 8 hours (slower, safer rate).

cards.front: What are the initial management steps for shock in severe dehydration?
cards.back: Stop bleeding (if applicable), give oxygen, keep warm, obtain IV or IO access (take blood and DEFG cultures), fix cannula and immobilize extremity if applicable, consider antibiotics for suspected sepsis, then proceed to fluid bolus therapy.

cards.front: What happens if a child with severe dehydration is not getting better after initial management?
cards.back: Reassess for shock and treat if required. Consider giving fluid faster. Seek senior advice. Consider antibiotics if sepsis suspected. Check glucose level. Assess and treat severe malnutrition if present.

cards.front: What is the summary of management for severe dehydration?
cards.back: Treat shock if present. Give oral fluids and if ineffective proceed to IV fluids. Once severe dehydration has been corrected, give maintenance fluids by oral route if possible, or IV if not tolerated. Check glucose in a child with severe malnutrition.

cards.front: Case 1: Child 7.3 kg with severe dehydration, no malnutrition, needs ORS via NG/oral every 15 minutes. What is the dose?
cards.back: Plan A: 5 ml/kg per 15 minutes = 5 x 7.3 = 36.5 ml per 15 minutes (use weight band = 40 ml/15 min). Reassess in 1-2 hours for improvement.

cards.front: Case 2: Child 7.3 kg with severe dehydration, no malnutrition, oral rehydration fails. What is the IV dose and drip rates?
cards.back: Plan B: 20 ml/kg/hour x 4 hours = 20 x 7.3 = 146 ml/hr (weight band 160 ml/hr). Using 60 drops/ml dropper: 146-160 drops per minute. Using 15 drops/ml dropper: 34-40 drops per minute.

cards.front: Case 3: Child 11 kg with severe dehydration, severe malnutrition, drip fails. What is the IV dose and drip rates?
cards.back: Plan: 10 ml/kg/hour x 8 hours = 10 x 11 = 110 ml/hr (weight band 80 ml/hr). Using 60 drops/ml dropper: 80 drops per minute. Using 15 drops/ml dropper: 20 drops per minute. Duration is 8 hours (not 4 hours due to malnutrition).

cards.front: Case 4: Child 3.1 kg with severe dehydration, no malnutrition, vomiting everything. What is the IV dose and drip rates?
cards.back: Plan B: 20 ml/kg/hr x 4 hours = 20 x 3.1 = 62 ml/hr (round to 60 ml/hr). Using 60 drops/ml dropper: 62-60 drops per minute. Using 15 drops/ml dropper: 15 drops per minute. WARNING: ensure no more than 100 ml can run through suddenly.

cards.front: Case 5: Child 10 kg with severe dehydration, no malnutrition, cannot get IV access, not getting better - requires NG infusion. What is the dose and rates?
cards.back: Plan: 20 ml/kg/hour x 6 hours via NG tube = 20 x 10 = 200 ml/hr (weight band 240 ml/hr). Using 60 drops/ml dropper: 200-240 drops per minute. Using 15 drops/ml dropper: 50-60 drops per minute.

cards.front: What are important cautions when using manual infusion controllers and rollers for drip rates?
cards.back: Manually set roller controllers tend to slow down progressively. Dial flows rate depends on height above infusion site. Both are affected by position of limb/drip site if this occludes or alters vascular resistance. You cannot reliably set and walk off as they do not alarm when going too fast or too slow. Must maintain constant vigilance and use protection to prevent catastrophic infusion of sudden large amounts of fluid.

cards.front: What is the formula for calculating drip rates from ml/hr if using 60 drops/ml dropper?
cards.back: If 60 drops per ml dropper: Volume per hour in ml = drops per minute (1:1 ratio). Example: 100 ml/hr = 100 drops per minute.

cards.front: What is the formula for calculating drip rates from ml/hr if using 15 drops/ml dropper?
cards.back: If 15 drops per ml dropper: Volume per hour in ml = drops per minute divided by 4. Example: 100 ml/hr = 100 ÷ 4 = 25 drops per minute.

cards.front: Where in the ETAT sequence does severe dehydration assessment occur?
cards.back: Severe dehydration assessment occurs AFTER Circulation/Coma/Convulsions evaluation — it is not the initial screening step.

cards.front: What additional samples should be collected when establishing IV/IO access in a shocked child?
cards.back: Collect DEFG cultures (Don't Ever Forget Glucose plus septic workup samples) at the same time as IV/IO access.

cards.front: What cardiac complication must be monitored for during and after fluid bolus administration?
cards.back: Congestive cardiac failure (CCF) — monitor during and after every bolus.

cards.front: Why can't severely malnourished children be rehydrated the same way as well-nourished children?
cards.back: They have precarious metabolic and circulatory status; standard fluid rates can precipitate cardiac failure. Use slower, lower-volume regimens.

cards.front: What two findings define severe malnutrition for fluid-management purposes?
cards.back: Visible severe wasting AND bilateral pitting oedema of both feet.

cards.front: If a child is improving after 2 hours of oral rehydration, what is the next step?
cards.back: Continue oral rehydration for another 2 hours (total 4 hours) before considering IV therapy.

cards.front: How should IV-to-oral transition be managed as dehydration resolves?
cards.back: Gradually slow the IV rate while increasing oral fluids — overlapping the two rather than abruptly stopping IV.

cards.front: How is ORS administered during initial rehydration — single bolus or divided dosing?
cards.back: Divided dosing: continuous infusion OR 5 mL/kg every 15 minutes. NOT a single 20 mL/kg bolus per hour.

cards.front: How should NG fluids be delivered — continuous or bolus?
cards.back: Continuous infusion is preferred over intermittent bolus dosing for better tolerance.

cards.front: What concentration of dextrose is used to treat hypoglycaemia during shock, and what dose?
cards.back: 10% dextrose at 5 mL/kg IV (not other concentrations).

cards.front: Why are electronic infusion pumps preferred over manual roller controllers?
cards.back: Electronic pumps prevent catastrophic overdose because the set rate is delivered regardless of drip height, limb position, or vascular resistance.

cards.front: How does the height of the drip bag affect flow rate with a manual controller?
cards.back: Higher bag → higher hydrostatic pressure → faster flow than set; lower bag → slower flow. Position changes alter actual delivered rate.

cards.front: How do limb position and vascular resistance affect manual drip rate?
cards.back: Occlusion or increased vascular resistance at the cannula site slows actual flow below set rate; relaxation/movement may speed it up.

cards.front: What is the preventive ORS dose AFTER rehydration is complete (Plan A maintenance)?
cards.back: 10 mL/kg PO after each stool — not on a fixed schedule.

cards.front: For how long should a child be observed after completing rehydration to ensure maintenance?
cards.back: 6 hours of observation post-hydration.

cards.front: What is weight banding in paediatric fluid calculation?
cards.back: A practical shortcut: use the child's estimated weight band rather than an exact weight to look up volumes/doses, reducing calculation errors at the bedside.

cards.front: What are the four main components of emergency care for children?
cards.back:
1. Triage - Find the children who need emergency care
2. Provision of emergency care - Resuscitation/Life support
3. Provision of post resuscitation care
4. Stabilisation and transport
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Define triage in the context of paediatric emergency care.
cards.back: Triage is a sorting process that aims to find life-threatening problems. It is NOT about making a diagnosis.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What does ETAT stand for and who developed it?
cards.back: ETAT = E: Emergency, T: Triage, A: Assessment, T: Treatment. Developed by the World Health Organisation (WHO).
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What does the ABCC-C-D framework assess in a seriously ill child?
cards.back: A = Airway, B = Breathing, C = Circulation, C = Coma, C = Convulsion, D = Dehydration. This framework identifies life-threatening problems.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: List the six final pathways to death in children.
cards.back:
1. Respiratory obstruction
2. Respiratory depression
3. Alveolar or chest wall failure
4. Fluid loss
5. Fluid maldistribution
6. Heart failure
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name three conditions that cause respiratory obstruction in children.
cards.back: Croup, asthma, bronchiolitis
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name three conditions that cause respiratory depression in children.
cards.back: Post-ictal state, meningitis, poisoning
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name three conditions that cause alveolar or chest wall failure in children.
cards.back: Pulmonary contusion, flail chest, diaphragmatic hernia (traumatic or congenital)
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What are two causes of fluid loss that lead to the final pathway to death in children?
cards.back: Gastroenteritis and femoral or pelvic fractures
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name two conditions that cause fluid maldistribution in children.
cards.back: Meningococcal shock and anaphylaxis
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: Name two causes of heart failure that lead to the final pathway to death in children.
cards.back: Local anaesthetic toxicity and congenital heart disease
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-001-010.yml

cards.front: What is triage according to the lecture definition?
cards.back: Triage is a sorting process. It aims to be quick and find life-threatening problems. Triage is NOT making a diagnosis.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What does ETAT stand for?
cards.back: E = Emergency, T = Triage, A = Assessment, T = Treatment
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What is the ETAT system equivalent abbreviated as for triage assessment?
cards.back: ABCC-C-D: A = Airway, B = Breathing, C = Circulation, C = Coma, C = Convulsion, D = Dehydration
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: How do you assess if a child's airway is patent?
cards.back: Check: Is the child breathing? Is there noisy breathing (secretions, stridor, snoring, grunting)? Is the child blue?
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What are signs of severe respiratory distress in children?
cards.back: Fast breathing, severe chest-indrawing, grunting, difficulty feeding, and signs the child is getting tired
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What are the key assessment questions for airway and breathing?
cards.back:
1. Is the child breathing?
2. Is the airway obstructed?
3. Is the child blue?
4. Does the child have severe respiratory distress?
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: How do you assess circulation in a seriously ill child?
cards.back: Check: Does the child have warm hands? If not, is capillary refill >2 seconds? Is the pulse weak and fast?
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What is the AVPU scale for assessing consciousness?
cards.back: A = Alert, V = responds to Voice, P = responds to Pain, U = Unresponsive. Responds only to Pain or Unresponsive = 'coma'
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What are subtle signs of convulsion in neonates that may not be obvious?
cards.back: Eye flickering or deviation, hand or foot movements, mouth movements, repetitive movements, apnoea
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: How do you assess severe dehydration in a child with diarrhoea?
cards.back: Check: Is the child lethargic or unconscious? Does the child have sunken eyes? Does the skin pinch go back very slowly?
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: If a child has ANY sign of ABCC-C-D, what action should be taken immediately?
cards.back: Emergency treatment should be started immediately
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-011-020.yml

cards.front: What does the Priority Signs mnemonic 3TPR-MOB stand for?
cards.back:
3T: Tiny baby, Temperature, Trauma
P: Pallor, Poisoning, Pain (severe)
R: Respiratory, Restless, Referral (urgent)
MOB: Malnutrition, Oedema, Burns
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What age is considered a 'tiny baby' in triage?
cards.back: Less than 3 months of age
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What is the temperature cutoff for 'Temperature' as a priority sign?
cards.back: Temperature >38 degrees Celsius (hot to touch)
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: How do you assess for severe pallor as a priority sign?
cards.back: Compare the child's palm with the mother's palm. Severe pallor indicates severe anaemia.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What is the assessment method for malnutrition as a priority sign?
cards.back: Look rapidly at arms, legs, and chest for severe wasting (Marasmus)
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What is the clinical sign of oedema as a priority sign?
cards.back: Oedema of BOTH feet, with or without hair and skin changes (suggesting Kwashiorkor). May indicate heart failure, kidney problems, or liver problems.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What are the three 'R' priority signs and their assessment?
cards.back:
1. Respiratory (NOT severe) - judgment/experience
2. Restless - lethargy or irritability; 'V' on AVPU; continuously irritable, crying, inconsolable
3. Referral - urgent referral
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What does 'E' stand for in the EPQ triage action system?
cards.back: E = Emergency. Take the child to the emergency treatment area.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What does 'P' stand for in the EPQ triage action system?
cards.back: P = Priority. Take the child to the front of the queue.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: What does 'Q' stand for in the EPQ triage action system?
cards.back: Q = Queue. No emergency or priority signs present; non-urgent cases can wait their turn.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: When should triage be performed and where?
cards.back: Triage should be performed as soon as the child arrives, BEFORE reception/administration. Locations include: outpatients queue, emergency room, ward, or emergency treatment area (room/trolley with access to drugs and supplies).
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: Who can perform triage?
cards.back: ALL STAFF can do triage: nurses, doctors, clerks, gatekeepers, and cleaners.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-021-030.yml

cards.front: Define the AVPU scale for rapid assessment of conscious level.
cards.back: A = Alert, V = Responds to Voice, P = Responds to Pain, U = Unresponsive. Responds only to Pain or Unresponsive indicates coma.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: What is coma in the context of AVPU assessment?
cards.back: Responds only to Pain or Unresponsive = 'coma'
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: How do you detect convulsions in a child?
cards.back: Look for generalised convulsions (easy to see) or subtle signs especially in neonates: eye flickering or deviation, hand or foot movements, mouth movements, repetitive movements, or apnoea.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: What are the three key questions to assess dehydration in a child with diarrhoea?
cards.back:
1. Is the child lethargic or unconscious?
2. Does the child have sunken eyes?
3. Does the skin pinch go back very slowly?
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 1: A three-year old girl is in the queue. Airway and breathing OK. Hot but cold hands. Capillary refill 1.5 seconds. Alert. Had diarrhoea (4 loose stools/day). Skin pinch takes 3 seconds. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: P (Priority) - Signs of moderate dehydration (cold hands, abnormal capillary refill, slow skin pinch) warrant priority placement but not emergency treatment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 2: A four-year old male was rushed in. Convulsed 1 hour ago. Fast breathing, no cyanosis, no respiratory distress. Very hot, responds quickly to questions. No diarrhoea or vomiting. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: P (Priority) - Recent seizure with fever (possible febrile convulsion) warrants priority but not emergency given current alertness and normal breathing.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 3: A one-year old had seizure at home, then again outside clinic. Now unconscious. Breathing wet and noisy, drooling. Looking blue. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: E (Emergency) - Coma (unresponsive), airway compromise (wet breathing, drooling), cyanosis, and recurrent seizures all indicate immediate emergency treatment needed.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 4: A two-year old male is rushed to clinic acutely convulsing. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: E (Emergency) - Active convulsion at presentation is an emergency sign requiring immediate intervention.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 5: A nine-month girl and brother playing with old bead necklace. Child suddenly choking, brought to you by nurse. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: E (Emergency) - Choking indicates airway obstruction requiring immediate emergency treatment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 6: A three-year old boy (14kg). Pale, floppy, difficulty breathing. Unwell 3 days, coughing. Fast breathing, heavy severe chest indrawing. Airway patent. Alert. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: E (Emergency) - Severe respiratory distress (severe chest indrawing, difficulty breathing, pallor, floppiness) is an emergency sign.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 7: A four-month old baby (5kg). Fever, rapid breathing, refusing feeds. 2 episodes vomiting and watery diarrhoea. Cold hands. Capillary refill 6 seconds. Femoral pulse fast and weak. No chest indrawing, no respiratory noises. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: E (Emergency) - Signs of severe dehydration and circulatory shock (cold hands, capillary refill >2 seconds, weak fast pulse, fluid loss) indicate emergency treatment needed.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-031-040.yml

cards.front: Scenario 8: An 18-month old boy (11kg). Unwell 2 days, feverish. Abdominal pain, fast breathing. Airway fine, no chest indrawing. No diarrhoea history. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: Q (Queue) - No emergency signs (airway clear, breathing present but not distressed) and no priority signs. Can wait their turn in queue.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 9: A 10-week old baby (3.5kg). Hot, crying a lot, will not suckle. Weight 3.5kg. Airway, breathing, circulation normal. No diarrhoea or dehydration. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: P (Priority) - Tiny baby (<3 months) with fever is a priority sign requiring early assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 10: A 14-month old girl. Sleeping all day. Irritable when awoken, rouses to loud voice/arm shaking. Warm hands but look pale. No diarrhoea or vomiting. Deep breathing, lower chest wall indrawing. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: P (Priority) - Responds to voice (V on AVPU indicating altered consciousness) and respiratory distress (chest wall indrawing) warrant priority assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 11: A one-year old girl (6.5kg). 2-day history diarrhoea and vomiting. Restless and irritable. Airway and breathing OK. Warm hands. AVPU=voice. Skin pinch 4 seconds. Sunken eyes. Skin and bones appearance. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: P (Priority) - Severe malnutrition (skin and bones, wasting) and moderate-severe dehydration (sunken eyes, slow skin pinch) are priority signs.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 12: A 4-year old boy with sore ear, well-appearing and playful. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: Q (Queue) - No emergency or priority signs. Non-urgent case can wait their turn.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 13: A 6-year old boy complaining of chest pain. Breathing normally, not shocked, conversant. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: Q (Queue) - No emergency or priority signs. Well appearance and normal vital signs indicate non-urgent case.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 14: A 7-week old boy with fever 1 day. Looking around, breathing normally, no shock or dehydration. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: P (Priority) - Tiny baby (<3 months) with fever is a priority sign requiring early assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 15: A 7-year old girl with asthma presenting with mild wheeze, bright and alert. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: Q (Queue) - Mild wheeze without respiratory distress and with maintained alertness is non-urgent. Can wait their turn.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 16: An 18-month girl with cough and runny nose, barking cough, and stridor. She is agitated. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: E (Emergency) - Stridor indicates potential airway obstruction requiring emergency treatment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Scenario 17: A 2-year old girl who won't walk 1 day. Crying but consolable, breathing normally, not shocked. Triage as E/P/Q.
cards.back: Q (Queue) - Normal breathing, normal circulation, and consolability indicate no emergency or priority signs. Non-urgent case can wait.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: What is the summary framework for effective triage?
cards.back:
1. Look for Emergency signs (ABCC-C-D)
2. Move to ER and treat any signs found
3. Look for Priority signs (3TPR-MOB)
4. Place priority patients at front of queue
5. Move on to next patient
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: What does the proverb say about learning emergency care?
cards.back: The best time to start learning about emergency care for children is ten years ago. The next best time is now.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-041-050.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 1 (Answers): A 3-year old with hot child, cold hands, cap refill 1.5s, diarrhoea 4x/day, skin pinch 3s. Why is this Priority?
cards.back: Moderate dehydration signs (cold hands, prolonged capillary refill, slow skin turgor) indicate priority assessment is needed but not emergency-level shock.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 2 (Answers): A 4-year old convulsed 1 hour ago, fast breathing, no cyanosis, very hot, responds quickly. Why is this Priority?
cards.back: Recent seizure with fever (likely febrile convulsion) and elevated temperature are priority signs, but maintained alertness and normal oxygenation prevent emergency classification.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 3 (Answers): A 1-year old with recurrent seizures, now unconscious, wet breathing, drooling, blue. Why is this Emergency?
cards.back: Multiple emergency signs present: coma (unresponsive), airway obstruction signs (wet breathing, drooling), cyanosis, and recurrent convulsions all require immediate emergency intervention.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 4 (Answers): A 2-year old actively convulsing at presentation. Why is this Emergency?
cards.back: Active convulsion at the time of presentation is an emergency sign requiring immediate airway assessment and seizure management.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 5 (Answers): A 9-month girl choking on bead necklace. Why is this Emergency?
cards.back: Choking represents acute airway obstruction (A in ABCC-C-D) and requires immediate emergency airway intervention.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 6 (Answers): A 3-year old (14kg) pale, floppy, difficulty breathing, 3-day cough, severe chest indrawing. Why is this Emergency?
cards.back: Severe respiratory distress (B in ABCC-C-D) evidenced by severe chest indrawing, difficulty breathing, pallor, and floppiness indicate emergency-level breathing compromise.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 7 (Answers): A 4-month baby (5kg) fever, rapid breathing, refusing feeds, cold hands, cap refill 6s, weak fast pulse, diarrhoea/vomiting. Why is this Emergency?
cards.back: Signs of severe dehydration and circulatory shock (C in ABCC-C-D): cold hands, capillary refill >2 seconds, weak fast pulse, fluid loss indicate emergency-level shock requiring immediate resuscitation.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 8 (Answers): An 18-month boy (11kg) feverish 2 days, abdominal pain, fast breathing, airway fine, no chest indrawing, no diarrhoea. Why is this Queue?
cards.back: No emergency signs (ABCC-C-D all absent) and no priority signs. Fever is present but child is alert and breathing is fast but not distressed - non-urgent.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 9 (Answers): A 10-week old (3.5kg) crying, won't suckle, hot, airway/breathing/circulation normal, no dehydration. Why is this Priority?
cards.back: Tiny baby (<3 months) with fever is a priority sign (3T: Tiny baby + Temperature) requiring priority-level assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 10 (Answers): A 14-month girl sleeping all day, irritable when awoken, rouses to voice/shaking, warm hands but pale, deep breathing, chest indrawing. Why is this Priority?
cards.back: Altered consciousness (V on AVPU - responds to voice only) and respiratory distress (chest wall indrawing, deep breathing) are priority signs.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-51-60.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 11 (Answers): A 1-year old (6.5kg) 2-day diarrhoea/vomiting, restless irritable, warm hands, AVPU=V, skin pinch 4s, sunken eyes, skin and bones. Why is this Priority?
cards.back: Severe malnutrition (skin and bones appearance) is a priority sign (MOB: Malnutrition). Also moderate-severe dehydration (sunken eyes, slow skin turgor) with altered consciousness requires priority assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 12 (Answers): A 4-year old with sore ear, well-appearing and playful. Why is this Queue?
cards.back: No emergency signs (ABCC-C-D) and no priority signs (3TPR-MOB). Well-appearing and playful indicate non-urgent case can wait their turn.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 13 (Answers): A 6-year old with chest pain, breathing normally, not shocked, conversant. Why is this Queue?
cards.back: Normal breathing, normal circulation, maintained alertness and conversance indicate no emergency or priority signs. Non-urgent case can wait their turn.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 14 (Answers): A 7-week old with fever 1 day, looking around, breathing normally, no shock/dehydration. Why is this Priority?
cards.back: Tiny baby (<3 months) with fever (>38C) represents both tiny baby and temperature priority signs requiring early assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 15 (Answers): A 7-year old with asthma, mild wheeze, bright and alert. Why is this Queue?
cards.back: No respiratory distress, mild symptoms only, and maintained alertness indicate non-emergency asthma. No emergency or priority signs present.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 16 (Answers): An 18-month girl with cough, runny nose, barking cough, stridor, and agitation. Why is this Emergency?
cards.back: Stridor (noisy breathing indicating airway obstruction) is an emergency sign (A in ABCC-C-D, or potential croup). Requires emergency airway assessment.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: Practice Scenario 17 (Answers): A 2-year old who won't walk 1 day, crying but consolable, breathing normally, not shocked. Why is this Queue?
cards.back: Normal breathing, normal circulation, and consolability (not continuously irritable) indicate no emergency or priority signs. Non-urgent case can wait their turn.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: What is the key clinical difference between Emergency (E) and Priority (P) triage categories?
cards.back: Emergency: Child has signs of ABCC-C-D (airway, breathing, circulation, coma, convulsion, dehydration) or other life-threatening conditions requiring immediate treatment. Priority: Child has concerning signs (3TPR-MOB) that require early assessment but not immediate emergency intervention.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml

cards.front: What vital sign changes suggest a child is moving into shock (circulatory compromise)?
cards.back: Cold hands, capillary refill >2 seconds, weak or absent pulses, weak and fast pulse (weak at wrist, fast rate), and altered consciousness.
cards.source: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70
cards.sourceFile: Emergency care for children- the seriously ill child_pages-61-70.yml
